segments.s669-g41: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the time clause. نیمی زتیره شب is a genitive phrase 'half of the dark night' (نیمی 'half' + زتیره 'of the darkness/blackness' + شب 'night'). اندر گذشت places the verb after the locative particle (اندر = 'in/within') and گذشت is the past tense 'passed/elapsed'.
segments.s669-g42: سپهدار جنگی is the subject phrase 'the warlike commander' (سپهدار = commander; جنگی as adjectival 'warlike'). برون شد is a two-word verbal expression meaning 'went out/issued forth' (برون = 'out/forth', شد = past of شدن used intransitively). به دشت is a directional complement 'onto the plain'.
segments.s669-g43: سپهبد is the subject (spahbed, a high-ranking commander). بران سوی لشکر کشید is a verbal phrase: بران ('to/at that') + سوی لشکر ('toward the army') + کشید, here meaning 'drew/advanced' (کشیدن used for moving or deploying troops). The order is subject + directional/target phrase + verb.
segments.s669-g44: زترکان طلایه is a genitive construction 'the vanguard of the Turks' (ز = poetic 'of/from'). کس ... ندید is an idiomatic negative indefinite: کس = 'no one' and ندید = 'did not see', with او را (object marker + pronoun) marking 'him' as the object.
segments.s669-g45: چو آمد begins a temporal clause 'when he came'. به نزدیک‌ی رزمگاه is a locative phrase 'to/near the battlefield' (به = 'to/toward', نزدیک‌ی = 'near/nearby', رزمگاه = 'battlefield'). Word order is verb after the temporal particle followed by the directional phrase.
segments.s669-g46: دم نای رویین is a noun phrase 'the breath/sound of the iron reed (flute)' (دم = 'breath/sound of', نای = 'ney, reed flute', رویین = 'iron-made/iron-coated'). برآمد is the past intransitive 'arose/issued forth', and ز راه is the source 'from the road' (ز = poetic 'from').
segments.s669-g47: چو introduces another temporal clause 'when'. آواز کوس آمد is 'the sound/call of the drum came' (آواز = 'sound/call', کوس = 'war-drum', آمد = 'came'). و connects the next phrase, کرنای here refers to the trumpet/blast (a nominal phrase that will link to the following verb).
segments.s669-g48: بجستند is a past plural verb 'they sprang/leapt up' (here describing sudden movement). ترکان جنگی is the subject 'the warlike Turks' and ز جای is the adverbial origin 'from their places'. The verb agrees with the plural subject.
segments.s669-g1: ستاره شمر گفت بهرام را is a clause meaning 'the star of Shamr said/told Bahram'. ستاره شمر is a genitival phrase ('the star of Shamr'); گفت is the speech verb placed after the subject (classical word order). بهرام را uses را as the (definite) object/target marker showing Bahram is the person addressed/affected by the saying.
segments.s669-g2: که در چارشنبه مزن گام را is the content clause introduced by که ('that/which' → here introducing reported speech/command). در چارشنبه is a temporal prepositional phrase 'on/at Wednesday', مزن is a prohibitive imperative 'do not strike/do not tread', and گام را 'the step' is the definite object marked by را. The verb (مزن) gives the command and the enclitic را on گام marks the definite object.
segments.s669-g3: اگر زین به پیچی گزند آیدت is a conditional protasis starting with اگر 'if'. زین 'this saddle' is the theme; به پیچی uses به + پیچی to indicate manner/cause ('by a twist' / 'if it turns'), گزند is the harm, and آیدت is the verb 'comes to you' where the clitic -ت marks 'to you'. The verb ends the clause as is typical in Persian.
segments.s669-g4: همه کار ناسودمند آیدت is the apodosis (result clause). همه کار 'all (your) affairs' is the subject, ناسودمند is a predicative adjective 'unprofitable/fruitless' placed before the verb, and آیدت 'will come upon you' (again with -ت 'to you') expresses the result; word order is subject + predicate-adjective + verb.
segments.s669-g5: یکی باغ بد در میان سپاه is an existential/indefinite construction: یکی 'one/a certain' + باغ بد 'an ugly/bad garden' followed by the locative phrase در میان سپاه 'in the midst of the army'. The adjective بد follows the noun (classical Persian allows adjective after noun), and در میان forms the locative 'in the middle of'.
segments.s669-g6: ازین روی و زان روی بد رزم‌گاه is a paired locative: ازین روی 'from/on this side' and زان روی 'from/on that side' joined by و. ین here is the orthographic variant of این ('this'), and روی is 'side/surface'. بد rasm-gāh combines as 'a bad/harsh battlefield' with the adjective بد qualifying رزم‌گاه; the whole phrase describes battlefields lying on both sides of the garden.
segments.s669-g7: بشد چارشنبه هم از بامداد: بشد is a classical perfective/punctual verb meaning 'it became/it happened' (here 'when it became Wednesday'); چارشنبه is the time expression 'Wednesday', هم adds the sense 'also/too' or emphatic 'even', and از بامداد 'from dawn' gives the starting time. Together they set the temporal frame ('When Wednesday arrived, even from dawn...').
segments.s669-g8: بدان باغ کامروز باشیم شاد contains an archaic locative/compound: بدان (classical contraction of به + آن = 'in/at that') + باغ 'garden'; کامروز 'that day/this very day' specifies the day, and باشیم شاد is a first-person-plural subjunctive/optative or narrative form 'we were/let us be happy' — here used narratively as 'we were merry'. The predicate adjective شاد follows the verb.
segments.s669-g105: چو برهم نهاده is a temporal/conditional phrase: چو (here fused as چوبرهم) = “when/once” + برهم (together) + نهاده (past participle of نهادن ‘to place’), so literally “when (things) were placed together.” بد here is an adverbial/qualifying element before انبوه; گشت is the past copula/verb ‘became.’ The whole segment says that by being placed together they turned into something dense/large. Note the verb گشت (past) takes the preceding nominal phrase as its subject.
segments.s669-g106: ببالا is an adverbial phrase ‘upward / in height’; و = 'and' linking two dimensions; پهنا = 'breadth / expanse' (a noun); یکی + کوه = ‘one / a single mountain’ (یکی modifies کوه); گشت is the past tense ‘became.’ Word order is adverb/adverbial + coordinating conjunction + noun phrase + predicate verb (گشت) indicating change of state.
segments.s669-g107: مرآن جای را is a demonstrative + noun + object marker construction meaning ‘that place’ (مرآن = a fused demonstrative phrase modifying جای; را marks جای as definite object/topic). نامداران یل is a noun phrase 'the renowned/illustrious heroes' with نامداران qualifying یل. The phrase as a whole identifies the place which is being named.
segments.s669-g108: همی is a habitual/narrative particle here giving a continuous or customary sense (‘each/always’); هرکسی is the subject 'each person / everyone'; خواند is the past verb 'called / named' (here 'to call/name' rather than 'to read'); بهرام تل is the name given (بهرام = proper name, تل = 'mound'). Word order: adverbial particle + subject + verb + object/name.
segments.s669-g109: سلیح سواران is a nominal phrase meaning 'armed riders/horsemen' (سلیح here stands with سواران to indicate weapons/armament or ‘armed’ riders); وچیزی 'and something/whatever else' links an additional element; که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; دید is the past tense verb 'saw / perceived.' Together this segment names the actors and adds a relative clause describing 'something they saw.'
segments.s669-g110: بجایی که is a purposive/locative phrase 'to the place that / to the spot which...' (بجایی = به+جایی, 'to/into the place'), که introduces the subordinate clause; بد سوی آن تل کشید contains a direction phrase toward آن تل ('toward that mound') and کشید is a verb of motion (here 'moved/drew'); the whole segment says they moved toward the place that faced/led toward that mound. Note: some classical spellings (بد for به) and archaic verb forms appear.
segments.s669-g111: یکی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone / a certain one'; نامه is the direct object 'a letter'; بنوشت is the past tense verb 'wrote' (classical form, here used as 'wrote [a letter]'); زی شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'to the king' (زی = از/to/of in classical usage determining the addressee or topic). Word order: subject (implicit 'one') + object + verb + indirect phrase indicating recipient.
segments.s669-g112: ز is the preposition 'from / concerning / about' (classical usage); پر here functions as 'full/of' (a qualifier); موده is a noun whose exact meaning in this line is archaic/contextual (here part of a descriptive phrase) and is best read together with the following words as describing the many banners/forces; و = 'and'; لشکر = 'army/host'; بی‌شمار = 'countless / innumerable.' The whole segment is a descriptive phrase giving the content of the letter (many banners and a countless army).
segments.s669-g153: به درگاه شاهت is a prepositional phrase ('to/at the threshold/court of your king') placed before the predicate for emphasis. می‌انجی is the predicate describing the speaker (here acting as 'intercessor'/'mediator'); منم is an emphatic copula ('I am') that follows the verb, reinforcing the subject. The word order (prepositional phrase → verb → copula) is typical poetic Persian where the location/role is fronted for prominence.
segments.s669-g154: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'), linking the preceding statement to an appositive explanation of the speaker's role. بر شهر ایران marks the scope ('over/upon the city of Iran'), with بر as the preposition and شهر ایران as its object. گوانجی is a role or name applied to the speaker; منم repeats the emphatic 'I am' to identify the speaker with that role.
segments.s669-g155: تو را is the topicalized/direct-object phrase marked by را ('you' as the one affected). بر همه مهتران indicates the comparison/limit ('above/over all the great ones'); مهتران here functions as 'great ones/chiefs'. مه is a predicate element (figuratively 'the moon' or 'great one') and کنم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used causatively — 'I make/raise' (i.e., 'I will make you ...').
segments.s669-g156: از اندیشه و رای تو is an ablative/source phrase ('from/by your thought and counsel') that supplies the means or basis for the action. و simply links the two nouns. به here functions adverbially ('by/through') before the verb کنم, which repeats the first-person action 'I will do/bring about' — together: 'I will do it by your thought and counsel.'
segments.s669-g157: ور is the conditional 'if'. ایدون here is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'is/comes/exists now' (it situates the secret as present). که introduces the subordinate clause identifying that thing as a secret (رازی‌ست) and نزدیک تو places it 'near you' — word order places the condition and its locator before the consequent.
segments.s669-g158: که again introduces the relative/explanatory clause ('which/that'). روشن is used adjectivally/verbally in the sense 'to enlighten/make bright'; کند is the present/subjunctive/optative form of کردن ('may/might do' or 'can do' in context). جان تاریک تو ('your dark soul') is the object being acted upon, with جان as 'soul/life' and تاریک as the adjective 'dark'.
segments.s669-g159: گشاده کن is an imperative construction: گشاده ('open/clear/frank') + کن (imperative 'do') = 'open/reveal'. آن راز is the direct object 'that secret'. و با من بگوی is an instruction to 'speak/tell with me' — syntactically 'با من' ('to/with me') precedes the verb; بگوی is a verb form 'tell/say!' (here an imperative/request).
segments.s669-g160: چوکارت is a fused interrogative/relative phrase ('what has happened to you' / 'what is your state/deed'), followed by چنین گشت ('thus became' — گشت 'became/was turned'), which reports the condition. دوری مجوی is a prohibitive formula: دوری ('separation/distance') is the noun and مجوی is the negative imperative/ prohibitive 'do not seek' (from جستن) — together: 'do not seek distance' or 'do not withdraw.'
segments.s669-g193: همی (an emphatic adverb) precedes the noun to mean 'always' or 'continually'. ترس is the noun 'fear' and is governed by the preposition از ('fear of'), followed by the demonstrative phrase ازین برگراینده دهر = 'of this one who turns/reverses the age'; برگراینده is an active-participle nominalized form ('the one who turns/overturns') modifying دهر ('the age/world'). Word order is typical Persian: adverb → noun → prepositional phrase describing the cause of the fear.
segments.s669-g194: مگر here functions as 'lest'/'unless' introducing the feared result. سازد is a present/subjunctive or optative 3rd-person singular of ساختن used in the sense 'to make/contrive'; its object is زهر ('poison'). بدین is the combination of ب (to) + این (this) meaning 'to/at this', and پای زهر 'the feet/base of (the) poison' is a locative phrase — idiomatically 'place poison at these feet' or 'set a poisoned device nearby.' The clause thus expresses the feared action by the reverser of time.
segments.s669-g195: کسی را که is a relative/topic phrase 'one who...' with را marking the person as definite/known. خون ریختن is a verbal noun phrase 'the shedding of blood' and پیشه گشت is a causal/periphrastic expression meaning 'became a profession' (پیشه = profession, گشت = became). The whole segment means 'someone for whom blood-shedding became his trade'.
segments.s669-g196: دل دشمنان (literally 'the heart of the enemies') is a genitive noun phrase meaning 'the enemies' hearts'. پر ز اندیشه گشت is a short predicate: پر ز = 'full of', اندیشه = 'thought/intent/plot', and گشت = 'became'. Together: 'the enemies' hearts became full of thought/plots.'
segments.s669-g197: بریزند is a classical third-person plural form (optative/indefinite or narrative present) meaning 'they pour' or 'they will pour'. خونش is 'his blood' (direct object). بران هم نشان is a locative/adverbial phrase: بران = 'on/thereon/at that', هم نشان = 'the same sign/token/mark'. The segment states that they will spill his blood on that same sign/place.
segments.s669-g198: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that', او is 'he', and ریخت (here) functions with خون to mean 'shed/poured (blood)' — a usage of the verbal root ریختن = 'to pour, to shed'. سر سرکشان is a genitive construction: سر = 'head' and سرکشان refers to the proud/rebellious ones (i.e. rebels); thus 'he shed blood on the heads of the rebels' or 'he caused the rebels' heads to be bloodied.'
segments.s669-g199: گر is the conditional 'if'. از شهر ترکان is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the city of the Turks' (i.e., in the Turks' city). برآری is a 2nd/3rd-person form of برآوردن/برآوردن-like root meaning 'bring about/produce' and here governs دمار ('ruin/calamity') as its object: 'if you bring ruin from the Turks' city' or more idiomatically 'if you lay waste to the Turks' city.'
segments.s669-g200: همی is the habitual/adverbial 'always'. کین بخواهند is a verb phrase 'they desire/seek vengeance' (کین = vengeance, بخواهند = 3rd pl. subjunctive/indicative of خواستن used narratively = 'they will want/seek'). فرجام کار is a genitive nominal phrase 'the outcome/end of the affair' and functions as the object/complement: 'they will seek vengeance as the outcome of the matter.'
segments.s669-g233: کنون is an adverb 'now' that sets the temporal frame. پیش + برترمنش forms a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the haughty one' (پیش = 'before/in front of' + برترمنش = 'the proud / haughty person'), and بنده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a servant' placed after that phrase by classical Persian word order (preposition + complement, then the noun introduced as the topical/contrasted figure). The -ای on بنده indicates indefiniteness ('a servant').
segments.s669-g234: This segment apposes and further describes that same 'servant': سپهبد is a title ('spahbed/commander'), while سری گرد is a compound-like description using سری ('secret, hidden') + گرد (here verbal/agentive sense 'going about, roaming'), giving 'one who moves/roams in secrecy' or 'secret-roving'. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', linking to جوینده‌ای 'a seeker' (جوینده + indefinite -ای). The sequence is appositional: title, descriptive compound, and a coordinated quality.
segments.s669-g235: چنان شد is a fixed narrative phrase 'it came to pass / it happened thus' (چنان 'thus/so' + شد past 'happened'). که introduces a content clause 'that ...' and the clause بر ما کند آفرین uses بر + ما ('upon us') as the object, with کند (3rd sing. present/subjunctive of کردن) functioning in narrative to mean 'he speaks/does' — here 'he utters/pays' — and آفرین is used nominally as 'praise/acclamation' (classical usage where a noun stands as the object of کند: 'does praise'). The combination gives 'it happened that he praised us.'
segments.s669-g236: This is a nominative/appositive phrase identifying the agent just mentioned: سپهدار and سالار are two close titles ('commander', 'chief/leader') followed by ترکان ('the Turks') and چین ('China') specifying which Turks (i.e., the Turks of China). The whole string names the person who performed the action in the previous clause.
segments.s669-g237: سپاس 'thanks/praise' followed by از + noun is a classical prepositional construction meaning 'praise to / thanks to' (سپاس از خداوند = 'thanks to the Lord'). خداوند خورشید و ماه is a genitival sequence 'the Lord of sun and moon' (titles/epithets following the lord). The whole is an independent exclamation 'Praise to the Lord of sun and moon.'
segments.s669-g238: This is a rhetorical question. کجا 'where' opens the question; داد is past 'gave' (he gave/granted); بر is the preposition 'upon/over/to' introducing the beneficiary or locus; بهتری is the comparative adjective 'better' (here spelled بهتر + ی as the adjectival/relational ending written as a separate token in the annotation), and دستگاه is the noun 'court/establishment'. Together: 'Where did He give (it) over a better court?' i.e. 'where else has He granted a better dominion?' Note that the detached ی is the written adjectival/relational element linking بهتر to دستگاه in classical orthography.
segments.s669-g239: بدرویش here functions as the indirect object 'to the dervish / to that poor one' (classical spelling reflecting به درویش), indicating the recipient; بخشیم is 1st plural present/subjunctive (used narratively) 'we gave/bestowed'; گنج کهن is the direct object 'an ancient treasure' (گنج 'treasure' + کهن 'ancient'). Word order is verb-medial as in classical Persian with recipient often before the verb phrase.
segments.s669-g240: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; پیدا شود is a two-word verbal phrase 'becomes found / is found' (پیدا 'found/visible' + شود 'becomes / is' — classical auxiliary), so 'when ... is found / when it becomes evident'. راستی زین سخن literally 'the truth of/from this speech' — راستی 'truth' and زین سخن 'of this speech' (زین = 'of this' in classical formulation, here written as a single token). The clause means 'when the truth of this account is revealed/evident.'
segments.s669-g49: زلشکر is an obscure/poetic noun left untranslated as a name/term here; بران سان is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in that manner' or 'thereupon' (بران = 'upon/thereon', سان = 'such/like'). برآمد is a past perfective verb 'arose/issued forth' and خروش is the noun 'a roar' (word order is poetic: adverbial + verb + noun, but meaning is 'a roar arose in that manner').
segments.s669-g50: که introduces a result or consequence clause ('such that/so that'). شیر ژیان را is the definite object phrase ('the living lion' or 'the lion of life') with را marking the object. بدرید is an old past form of 'to tear' (perfective) and گوش is 'ear' — together 'it tore the ear.' Word order here is clause introducer + object phrase + verb + object noun.
segments.s669-g51: به ... اندر is a doubled prepositional framing meaning 'into/within' (به ... اندر = 'into, in'); تاریکی is 'darkness'. دهاده is an adverbial/quantifying term ('in dozens/many' or 'numerous') modifying the verb. بخاست is the past perfective 'rose/rose up' (third-person plural implied by context).
segments.s669-g52: ز marks origin/source ('from'). دست چپ لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the army's left hand/left flank' (دست = 'hand/wing', چپ = 'left', لشکر = 'army'), and و connects the parallel phrase دست راست ('the right hand' or right flank). The phrase as a whole states the source/direction of the rising troops.
segments.s669-g53: یکی is the indefinite subject 'one' or 'one of them / each'. مر is an archaic/clitic element here not matching the listed modern senses (it functions like a reciprocal/emphatic particle in older poetry), so its exact modern gloss is uncertain; دگر را is the definite object 'the other'. ندانست is the past negative 'did not know/recognize.' باز after the verb functions adverbially as 'again' or 'anymore' (here: 'no longer / any more').
segments.s669-g54: شب تیره is a noun+adjective 'the dark night' giving the condition/cause; و links the coordinate noun phrase نیزه‌های دراز 'long spears' (نیزه‌های = 'spears' with plural suffix, دراز = 'long'). This clause supplies the reason why they could not recognize one another.
segments.s669-g55: بخنجر is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with a dagger' (ب– + noun = 'by/with'). همی is an older habitual/progressive particle meaning roughly 'continually/they were' or intensifier. آتش is 'fire' and افروختند is the past perfective plural 'they kindled/ignited.' The verb is plural past, showing the actors (soldiers) kindled fire.
segments.s669-g56: زمین و هوا را is the definite object phrase 'the earth and the air' (را marks the object), and همی‌سوختند is an imperfective/past-continuous form 'they were burning' or 'they burned' (commenting on ongoing destruction). Word order places the object before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s669-g281: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه بیامد is a past event: بیامد is the simple past 'came/arrived'. بر here is the older/pre-classical preposition meaning 'to/toward' and governs پهلوان ('the champion') as its goal.
segments.s669-g282: This clause has the predicate نامور شد ('became famed') with شد as the past/copular verb and نامور as the predicative adjective. دل پهلو functions as a phrase indicating the heart/side in question, and جوان is the subject ('the young man') placed after the verb for poetic emphasis: 'the young man's heart/side became renowned.'
segments.s669-g283: ازان is an archaic/deictic 'from that' (referring to the preceding letter). اندر شگفتی is a prepositional phrase 'in astonishment' (اندر = in), and بماند is the past/simple imperfect 'remained' (literally 'he stayed in that state').
segments.s669-g284: فرستاده و ایرانیان form a coordinated object/topic (only the second conjunct bears the object marker را). بخواند is a past tense verb here meaning 'read/recited (it)' to them (it can also mean 'summon' but in this narrative context it most naturally means 'read [the letter]').
segments.s669-g285: همان is the demonstrative 'that very'. خلعت شاه is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the king's robe' (older word order: noun before possessor). پیش here means 'in the presence (of)/before' and آورید is the imperative/plural or optative form 'bring (forth)'.
segments.s669-g286: برو here is an idiomatic/poetic particle introducing the ensuing reaction (literally 'go' in other contexts but used as 'thereupon/so' in poetry). آفرین is the exclamation 'bravo' and کرد is the past verb 'did/uttered', while هرکس که دید is a relative clause 'whoever saw (it)'. Together: 'each onlooker who saw it uttered “Bravo!”'.
segments.s669-g287: سخنهای ایرانیان is a genitive construction 'the words/utterances of the Iranians'. هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever/whatever there was', and بود is the past copula 'were/was' linking this idea to what follows.
segments.s669-g288: بران is an adverbial deictic 'on/thereupon' (referring back to that matter), نامه is the direct object 'the letter', اندر بدیشان is the prepositional phrase 'to them' (بدیشان = be + dishān, older form 'to them'), and نمود is the past verb 'showed/revealed'.
segments.s669-g241: شما is the 2nd-person plural/formal pronoun 'you' (subject). هم here means 'also' and follows the pronoun for emphasis. به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'to/unto God' (به + divine name), and نیایش کنید is a polite/plural exhortative (subjunctive/imperative) construction with the deverbal noun نیایش + کردن (کنید), meaning 'perform/pray' — literally 'do prayer'.
segments.s669-g242: همه = 'all/every' modifies نیکویی 'goodness/virtue'. در فزایش is a prepositional nominal phrase: در + فزایش ('in/increase' or 'for the increase of'), and کنید (same verb form as above) is an exhortation meaning 'bring about' or 'cause'. The clause as a whole is an imperative request: 'cause every goodness to increase.'
segments.s669-g243: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'. پهلوان را is the definite/direct object marked by the postposition را. بخواند is a 3rd-person narrative form (classical/past sense) from خواندن; here it means 'called' or 'summoned' (not 'read'), so the clause = 'the messenger summoned the champion.'
segments.s669-g244: بچربی is from چربیدن used idiomatically here as a verb 'to flatter' (classical narrative form with ب-). سخنها is the plural 'words/speeches' and فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'many/abundant', while براند (narrative form of راندن) here means 'sent forth/uttered' (to drive/put forth). Together: 'he uttered many speeches, flattering (him).' Note word order: verb-final narrative style with modifiers preceding the verb.
segments.s669-g245: کمر is the direct object 'girdle/belt'; خواست is the 3rd-person past of خواستن 'he wanted'. The adjectives پرگوهر ('full of gems / jeweled') and شاهوار ('regal, lofty') follow the noun they modify, which is typical in Persian, so the phrase reads 'he wanted a regal, gem-studded belt.'
segments.s669-g246: یکی is the numeral 'one/a single' and bاره here functions as 'instance/one piece' (classical use to indicate a single item or occasion). و is the coordinating 'and'. جامه زرنگار is a noun phrase: جامه 'garment/robe' + زرنگار 'gold-patterned / brocade', so the segment lists additional gifts: 'a single (one) [robe] and a gold-brocaded garment.'
segments.s669-g247: ستامی appears as a qualifier or epithet (here preserved in the text as an attributive form); بران is a locative/relational particle 'upon that / on it'. بارگی is a diminutive/derivative meaning 'leaf/leaf-like ornament', and پر ز زر is a classical idiom 'full of gold' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز = poetic form of 'from/of'; زر = 'gold'). Grammatically this is a noun phrase placed before the next clause: 'upon it, a leaf-like ornament full of gold.'
segments.s669-g248: به مهر is an instrumental/phrasing 'as a token of affection' (به + مهر where مهر here denotes 'love/affection' or a token thereof). مهره‌ای is 'a bead/amulet' (indefinite by the -ای), بر نشانده is a compound verbal phrase: بر (on/upon) + نشانده (perfect participle 'placed/set'), and گهر is 'a gem/jewel' (the object placed). The clause describes placing a jeweled bead as a mark of affection: 'as a token of affection he placed a bead and set a jewel upon it.'
segments.s669-g137: This is a rhetorical question. جهان جستن is a deverbal adjective phrase 'world‑seeking/ambitious' (جستن 'to seek' used adjectivally) that modifies شاه; ساوه names the city (Saveh) affiliated with the king. The order places the descriptive phrase before the noun-cluster to produce the literary epithet 'the world‑seeking king of Saveh.'
segments.s669-g138: A parallel interrogative clause continuing the lament. همه is a quantifier ('all/those many') applying to گنج, and دستگاه here means 'court/establishment' (the royal household). The coordinating و links the two nouns; کجا repeats the interrogative for rhetorical emphasis.
segments.s669-g139: Another rhetorical enumeration. همه quantifies the following nouns; پیل ('elephant') is literal or figurative for mighty beasts/troops. برگستوان is conjoined with پیل as a parallel noun phrase meaning roughly 'strong‑armed (men)' — an archaic compound used here as a martial epithet; its exact lexical sense is opaque in the given glosses but functions as a noun parallel to پیل.
segments.s669-g140: A second parallel question. بزرگان is the plural noun 'the great ones/nobles' and روشن‌روان is a compound epithet 'bright‑souled' or 'clear‑minded' that follows and qualifies بزرگان. The clause repeats کجا to maintain the mournful, listing rhythm.
segments.s669-g141: Yet another rhetorical list. همه quantifies the following items; تنبل literally 'lazy/sluggish' names a class or group (here likely scornful of the defeated or idle retainers), and جادوی (here a noun) means 'magic/sorcery.' The clause is elliptical: the nouns are presented without an explicit verb because the surrounding context supplies the lamenting question.
segments.s669-g142: Relative clause introduced by که ('which/that') referring back to the previous list. Persian poetry often omits the copula; here از ایشان تو بر یکسوی literally 'from them you to one side' = 'you are now apart from them' or 'you have set yourself apart from them.' بر یکسوی is an adverbial phrase 'to one side'; the present state is implied rather than explicitly verbalized.
segments.s669-g143: Statement with verbless/nominal predication followed by past copula: شهر ترکان 'the city of the Turks' is the topic, همی is an emphatic/adverbial intensifier ('indeed/entirely'), تو را marks the person concerned, and بس نبود ('was not enough') is the predicate in the past tense. The construction means 'that city was not sufficient for you.'
segments.s669-g144: چو introduces the circumstance ('when' / 'for when'); باب تو is a figurative expression ('your match/peer'—literally 'your gate/portal' used metaphorically), اندر جهان 'in the world' is a locative phrase, and کس نبود 'there was no one' is a negative existential in the past. Together: 'for there was no one in the world like you.'
segments.s669-g273: بدین نامه دیگر از من بخواه is an imperative clause. بدین (here written as ب + دین) functions as a prepositional/adverbial 'with this' or 'for this purpose' (classical spelling broken into parts); نامه is the noun 'letter'; دیگر 'another' modifies نامه. از من 'from me' marks the source; بخواه is the 2nd-person singular imperative of خواستن ('ask/request'), ordering the addressee to ask the speaker. Word order is typical Persian: adverbial phrase + direct object + source phrase + imperative verb.
segments.s669-g274: فرستیم چندانک باید سپاه contains the verb فرستیم (1st person plural form of فرستادن). Here it expresses 'we will send' / 'let us send' in the sense of a promise or intention. چندانک is an adverb meaning 'to that extent' / 'as many as' and sets the extent of what follows; باید is a modal 'must/it is necessary' and governs سپاه ('army, troops') as the thing required. The implied structure: 'we will send (فرستیم) (as many) troops as are necessary (چندانک باید سپاه)'.
segments.s669-g275: وز ایرانیان هرکه نزدیک تست begins with وز which in classical orthography often stands for و + از ('and from/of'), introducing a subset: 'and of the Iranians'. ایرانیان is the plural noun 'Iranians'; هرکه is a relative/indefinite 'whoever'; نزدیک + تست is a predicative phrase meaning 'is near you' where تست is an archaic clitic form (توست) 'you are' contracted onto the adjective. Word order places the group/provider first ('of the Iranians') then the restriction ('whoever is near you').
segments.s669-g276: که کردی همه راستی را درست is a relative clause modifying هرکه. که = 'whom/that'; کردی is the 2nd-person singular past tense of کردن ('you made/did'), taking همه راستی (literally 'all truth/uprightness') as its object; the object marker را follows همه راستی for definiteness/emphasis, and درست 'right/straight/upright' is a predicative adjective meaning 'upright' or 'made right.' The clause means 'whom you have made wholly upright/just.'
segments.s669-g277: بدین نامه در نام ایشان ببر is an imperative instruction. بدین نامه 'this/with this letter' (بدین = 'by/with this') is the direct object; در نام ایشان means 'in/under their name' or 'bearing their name'; ببر is the 2nd-person singular imperative of بردن 'take/carry/bring!' Here the verb is imperative placed at the end, a normal classical word order for commands.
segments.s669-g278: ز رنجی که بردند یابند بر is a purpose/result clause introduced by ز ('from/on account of') followed by رنجی ('a suffering/hardship'). که بردند is a relative clause 'that they bore/endured' (بردند is the perfect/past plural of بردن used here in the sense 'to bear/suffer'). یابند is the 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive ('may/find/obtain'), and بر ('on/upon/for') links the recompense/benefit implicitly to what follows or to the preceding context. Together the clause means 'so that for the suffering they endured they may obtain (reward/recompense).' The final object is implied rather than stated in this line.
segments.s669-g279: سپاه تو را مرزبانی دهم places the theme/object before the verb for emphasis. سپاه 'the army' is the object to be assigned; تو را is the recipient phrase 'to you' with the object marker را; مرزبانی is a noun meaning 'border-guard/service' or 'the office of guarding the frontier'; دهم is the 1st-person present/future of دادن 'I give/I will give'. Literal order: 'The army to you as border-guard I give.'
segments.s669-g280: تو را افسر و پهلوانی دهم repeats the beneficiary phrase 'to you' (تو را) and lists two things to be given: افسر and پهلوانی. افسر here is an insignia/headgear title (classical 'afsar' often a helmet or mark of office) and پهلوانی denotes 'pahlevani' — the status/virtue or office of a champion/hero. دهم is again 'I give' (1st person). The coordination 'افسر و پهلوانی' is a compound direct object; word order emphasizes the recipient before the verb.
segments.s669-g25: ازان (from that) + رخنه باغ (breach of the garden) is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb; classical Persian often puts location/source first. بیرون شدند is a past plural verbal phrase: بیرون (out) + شدند (past of شدن), here meaning 'they went out/they came out.'
segments.s669-g26: که introduces a subordinate/causal clause ('for/because'); دانست is a simple past 'he/it knew' with an implicit subject (common in epic narration). کان سرکشان functions as a nominal group ('those [rebel] ones') with کان an archaic demonstrative/particle and سرکشان the plural noun; چون شد(ند) asks 'how they became/what happened to them,' with چون = 'how' and شدند the past plural.
segments.s669-g27: برآمد ز در is a verbal phrase 'arose/issued from the gate' (برآمد = 'issued forth', ز = poetic 'from', در = 'gate/door'); ناله کرنای is a genitive construction 'the cry/wail of the trumpet' with ناله as the sound and کرنای specifying the instrument.
segments.s669-g28: سپهبد باسب is a noun phrase with the title سپهبد followed by the adverbial phrase باسب 'on horseback.' اندر آورد پای is a light verb construction: اندر آورد ('brought in/put in') + پای ('foot') = 'set his foot (in the stirrup)'; آورد is the past-tense main verb.
segments.s669-g29: سبک رخنه دیگر is a noun phrase where سبک qualifies رخنه as 'a light/quick breach' and دیگر = 'another.' اندر زدند is the past plural verb ('they struck/attacked in/within'), with اندر indicating the internal location.
segments.s669-g30: سپه را is the definite object (را marks 'the host/army' as the direct object). یکایک 'one by one' modifies the following verb phrase; بهم بر زدند is a compound verbal idea meaning 'they struck/overthrew into disorder/tore to pieces'—بهم here indicates 'into disorder/together', بر زدند is the past plural of زدن with the prefix بر showing direction/impact.
segments.s669-g31: هم 'also' + تاخت (past of تاختن) 'charged/rushed' gives the verb phrase; بهرام is the subject placed after the verb (permissible in classical word order). خشتی بدست is an instrumental phrase 'a brick in (his) hand' where بدست = 'in the hand' follows the object noun خشتی.
segments.s669-g32: چناچون 'thus/just so' opens the clause; بود is the past copula 'was/were'; مردم is the subject 'the people'; نیم مست is a two-word adjectival phrase 'half-drunk' (نیم = 'half/partly', مست = 'drunk' used figuratively as 'stupefied' or 'intoxicated').
segments.s669-g225: بفرمود is an honorific/past third-person verb meaning 'he said/ordered'; تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that'; نامه is the direct object 'the letter'; برخواندند is a past plural verb 'they read aloud' (بر- + خواندند, the prefix بر- here gives the sense 'read aloud/announce'). Together: 'he ordered that they read the letter.'
segments.s669-g226: بخواننده is a contracted dative 'to/for the reader' (به خواننده); بر is the preposition 'upon/onto' (here 'before/upon' in the sense of throwing onto someone); گوهر is 'gem/jewel'; افشاندند is past plural 'they scattered/they cast'. The clause literally says they threw/scattered jewels upon the reader.
segments.s669-g227: به آزادگان is an indirect-object prepositional phrase 'to/for the nobles'; گفت here functions as the speech verb 'he said' (the provided senses list gives noun senses, so the verbal sense is not listed); یزدان سپاس is a short formula meaning 'thanks/praise to God' (object or vocative). Word order: indirect object + verb + quoted content.
segments.s669-g228: نیاش (here a verbal noun or archaic verb meaning 'praise/thanksgiving') combines with کنـم (present subjunctive/volitional 'I will/do') to mean 'I will give praise' or 'I will render thanks'; من is the emphatic subject 'I'; بپیشش is a locative 'before him/in his presence' (به + پیش + -ش); سه پاس is the direct object 'three guards/sentries'. The clause conveys willingness/intention: 'I will (also) place three guards before him.'
segments.s669-g229: که introduces a content/relative clause 'that' (or 'who'); خاقان چین is the noun phrase 'the Khaghan of China' (چین = China in apposition); کهتر is a comparative/predicative adjective 'inferior/lesser'; ما is the comparative standard 'to us'; بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause means 'that the Khaghan of China was (made) lesser than us' or 'was beneath us.'
segments.s669-g230: سپهر بلند is a noun + adjective 'the lofty/heavenly sky'; افسر ما is 'our helmet' (افسر = helmet/crown, with the possessive pronoun ما following the noun); بود is the past copula 'was'. The order presents the sky as subject and the predicate 'our helmet', i.e. 'the lofty heavens were our helmet.'
segments.s669-g231: همی is an archaic/progressive particle marking continuous or habitual past action ('he began/was'); سر is 'head'; به چرخ فلک is a goal phrase 'to the wheel/vault of heaven' (چرخ فلک = sky/heaven); بر + فراخت is a past action 'lifted up' (فراخت is an older past form meaning 'raised/hoisted' — the lexeme sense as given is adjectival, so the verbal sense is irregular). The clause literally: 'he lifted his head up to the vault of heaven.'
segments.s669-g232: همی again marks the continuing past; خویشتن is the emphatic reflexive 'himself'; شاه گیتی is a two-word predicate 'king of the world' (شاه = king, گیتی = world); شناخت is the past verb 'he recognized/knew' (here used as 'he perceived/acknowledged himself as'). Word order places the reflexive object before the predicate and the past verb at the end, a common classical Persian arrangement.
segments.s669-g305: فرستاد is a third-person past tense verb 'sent'. و links two successive actions (here 'and then'). او را is the definite/object-marked pronoun 'him' (را marks the definite object). همانگه ز راه is an adverbial phrase: همانگه 'at that instant' + ز (poetic for 'from') + راه 'the road' — together 'immediately from the road.'
segments.s669-g306: پیاده is an adverb here meaning 'on foot' placed before the verb. بیاورد is the third-person form 'bring/has brought' (narrative past sense 'had him brought'). پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the army' (پیش = in front of/in the presence of; سپاه = army).
segments.s669-g307: چنین 'thus' is an adverb introducing reported speech. گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (third-person). پرموده is an epithet 'well‑taught/experienced' describing the speaker. او را here marks the indirect object 'to him' (the person being addressed). که begins the quoted content clause 'that / that I...'.
segments.s669-g308: سرافراز is a predicate adjective 'honored/triumphant'. بودم is the first-person past 'I was'. بهر is a preposition 'for/at' here indicating relation to an occasion/place. انجمن is the noun 'assembly/meeting' — together 'I was honored at the assembly.'
segments.s669-g309: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. بی‌منش 'without manners/ill‑mannered' and زینهاری 'an object of warning/reproof' are predicates describing the speaker's state; شدم is the past/resultative 'I became' (the subject 'I' is omitted, as is typical). The clause means 'now I have become ill‑mannered and an object of admonition.'
segments.s669-g310: ز is the preposition 'from/because of'. ارج 'honor/status' and بلندی 'loftiness/high rank' mark the previous elevated state; بخواری 'into humiliation/abasement' is the resulting state; شدم 'I became' completes the clause — 'from high honor I became humiliation/lowly.' This is a poetic balance: fall from honor into abasement.
segments.s669-g311: بدین روز is a temporal phrase 'on this day' (بدین = 'by/with this' = 'on this'). خود is emphatic 'yourself / yourself personally' (emphasizing the addressee). نیستی is 'you are not' (second‑person present/negative). خوش‌منش is the predicate 'courteous' — 'on this day you are not courteous.'
segments.s669-g312: که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/since/as' (here giving the circumstance). پیش آمدم is 'I came before/appeared in the presence (of you)' with آمدم 'I came.' ای is the vocative 'O!' and the repeated بد بد plus کنش is a condemnatory address: بد 'bad/evil' repeated for emphasis and کنش ('action' / 'doer' in context) forming a reproach 'O evil doer!' The whole gives an outraged address: 'when I came before you — O wicked doer!'
segments.s669-g9: ببردند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verb ‘they took/they carried off’. پرمایه is an adjective meaning ‘rich/abundant’ modifying an implied noun (goods/provisions). گستردنی is a participial/adjectival form derived from گستردن (‘to spread’), here describing the kind of provisions as ‘wide‑spreading’ or ‘that can be spread’; the sequence is verb + adjectival phrase describing what was taken.
segments.s669-g10: This is a nominal list (items taken) joined by و ‘and’. می‌و is an older spelling of میوه/میوَه ‘fruit’. رود here is best read as the musical sense (rūd, a stringed instrument) rather than a watercourse; it accompanies رامشگر (‘comforter’ or one who soothes) and خوردنی (‘edible/food’). The items are coordinated and follow the adjectival description in the previous segment.
segments.s669-g11: بیامد is simple past ‘he came’. the prepositional phrase بدان باغ (‘to that garden’) is formed from ب + دان + باغ (archaic/poetic compression for ‘to that garden’). می‌درکشید is an imperfect (continuous) form with the present/imperfect prefix می‑ plus the past stem درکشید — here meaning ‘was drawing in’ or ‘was inhaling/savoring’ (a continuous action at the time he entered).
segments.s669-g12: This clause continues with a simile: چوپاسی (‘like a shepherd/shepherding’) + ز ‘from’ + تیره شب ‘the dark night’ + اندر کشید. اندر + کشید is an older word order: اندر here means ‘into/within’ and کشید is past of کشیدن ‘to draw’; together they mean ‘drew in/from’. The whole phrase says he drew (it) in as a shepherd would from the dark night — a poetic image.
segments.s669-g13: طلایه is a noun ‘vanguard/forerunner’. بیامد is past ‘came’. بپرموده is a participial/compound form (ب‑ + پرموده) meaning ‘being instructed/instructed’ (a past participle/causal nuance) and modifies the subject; گفت is a past verb here ‘(he) said’. The sequence is subject + verbal/circumstantial modifiers + speech verb.
segments.s669-g14: که introduces reported speech (‘that’). بهرام را uses را as the object/topic marker marking Bahram as the referent of the remark. جام و باغست is a copular/equational phrase: جام (‘cup/wine’) and باغست (باغ + است contracted) ‘is/are the garden’ — here meaning they form a pair/are matched; جفت is the noun ‘pair/match’ completing the idea. Word order follows older poetic contraction (باغست = باغ است).
segments.s669-g15: سپهدار is the subject noun ‘the commander’ (a title). ازان (‘of/from those’) is a poetic contraction meaning ‘from those’ or ‘of those’. جنگیان is the plural/collective form related to جنگ (‘war/warriors’ here extended to mean ‘the warriors’); شش هزار follows as the numeral ‘six thousand’ that quantifies what the commander took.
segments.s669-g16: زلشکر is an older/compound form (likely ‘از لشکر’ or an obscure army‑term) introduced by ز‑ meaning ‘from the host/army’. گزین کرد is the light verb construction ‘selected/chose’ (گزین ‘choose’ + کرد ‘did’). گرد و سوار lists the kinds of troops: گرد here functions as part of a paired opposition with سوار — read in context as ‘foot/infantry’ (or ‘the bodies/host’) and سوار ‘horsemen, cavalry’; the coordinator و joins them.
segments.s669-g209: وزان پس (literally “from that then/after that”) is a temporal phrase meaning “from that time on.” در + گنج و دز gives the locative “in the treasury and (in) the stronghold/place (دز here names a place/fort),” and the clause ends with مر تو راست where مر = “to me” (object pronoun before the subject, an older/poetic order), تو = “you,” and راست = “true/faithful/is right” (predicate). The whole segment therefore says “from then on, in the treasury and stronghold you were true to me.”
segments.s669-g210: بدین is the fused prepositional phrase “by/with this” (ب + دین functioning as ‘by this’ in poetry). نامور بوم = “famed/renowned homeland” (نامور modifies بوم). کامت is کام + ـت, i.e. “your desire/wish” (possessive -t), and رواست = روا + ست, ‘is permitted/fitting’ (ست = copula). The clause means “with this famed homeland, your desire is permitted.”
segments.s669-g211: فرستاده آمد بگفت is a sequence of actions: “the messenger came and said/declared.” آمد is simple past “came,” and بگفت is the past narration “he said” (b- prefix plus گفت). این پیام is the direct object “this message.”
segments.s669-g212: زپیغام = ز + پیغام, literally “from/by the message/according to the report” (introducing the source of the news). بهرام شد شادکام = Bahram + شد (past ‘became’) + شادکام (predicate adjective ‘joyful/fortunate’). Together: “by the report Bahram became joyful.”
segments.s669-g213: نبشتند is the past third-person plural “they wrote/inscribed.” پس here is an adverb “then/thereupon.” نامه سودمند is the noun phrase “a useful/profitable letter” (نامه modified by سودمند). The segment reads “then they wrote a useful letter.”
segments.s669-g214: به نزدیک is the locative/directional “to the presence of / toward” (به + نزدیک used idiomatically). پیروز شاه بلند stacks epithets before the head noun: پیروز “victorious” modifies شاه “king,” and بلند is an honorific epithet “lofty/high.” So the phrase means “to the presence of the lofty, victorious king.”
segments.s669-g215: که introduces a subordinate clause “that.” خاقان چین is a title + place-name construction “the Khagan of China” (noun + modifier). زینهاری شدست uses زینهاری “a warning/admonition” as predicate and شدست (archaic perfect/past ‘has become/was’) — together meaning “had been warned” or “became admonished.”
segments.s669-g216: ز is the preposition “from/because of.” جنگ درازم groups ‘جنگ’ (war) with درازم (an old/poetic form of دراز, “long/lengthy”) to mean “the long war.” حصاری شدست = حصاری (fortification/fortress) + شدست (it became/was formed); the clause says “from the long war a fortress was made/was formed.”
segments.s669-g33: نجستند is past plural of جستن (here with the negative prefix ن-), literally 'did (not) leap/flee'; word order places the verb before its subject. گردان کس = 'anyone of the troop' (گردان 'troop' + کس 'person/one'), and از دست اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from his hand / from his control' with اوی an older possessive pronoun 'his'. Together: 'no one of his troop leapt from his hand' (= none escaped his control).
segments.s669-g34: به خون is an adverbial phrase 'into/with blood' (به + خون); گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' used here intransitively; یازان is an adjective 'gaping/open' modifying سر 'head'; شست is past 'washed' and اوی is the possessive 'his' referring to the agent. The clause describes the violent image: 'he washed the gaping heads in blood.'
segments.s669-g35: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth'. چکاچاک here denotes the metallic clink or clatter; و simply joins clauses; بانگ سران = 'the cry/shout of the leaders' (بانگ 'cry', سران 'leaders'). The whole segment names sounds that rose up.
segments.s669-g36: چو is a simile 'as/like'. پولاد را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'steel' as the material; پتک آهنگران is a noun phrase 'the sledgehammer of the blacksmiths' (پتک 'mallet/hammer', آهنگران 'blacksmiths') — together: 'like the blacksmiths' hammer on steel.'
segments.s669-g37: ازان باغ 'from that garden' (ازان = 'from that'); تا marks extent 'as far as/until'; جای 'place'; پرموده is an adjective before شاه meaning 'well‑taught/experienced' modifying شاه 'king'. The phrase sets the spatial span 'from that garden to the place of the experienced king.'
segments.s669-g38: تن 'body' combined with بی‌سران 'headless' yields 'headless bodies' (بی‌سران here adjectival/plural sense). بد is an adverb/adjective 'ill/poorly'; فگنده is a past participle 'thrown/laid'; به راه 'on/into the road' (به = 'into/towards'); the segment functions as a predicate describing how headless bodies lay 'ill‑thrown on the road.'
segments.s669-g39: چوآمد is a temporal conjunction+verb 'when (he/it) came'. بلشکر 'with an army' indicates the arriving force. گه خویش 'at his/their own time' (گه = 'occasion/time', خویش = 'one's own') and the enclitic ش marks possession ('his/its'); باز here functions as 'again' or 'as usual/returned'. The whole sets the time/context: 'when the army, at its accustomed moment, returned/arrived.'
segments.s669-g40: شبیخون is a compound noun 'night‑raid/surprise attack'. سگالید is a past transitive verb here meaning 'harried/assailed' (sense 1 rather than the literal 'set dogs on'), and گردن‌فراز is an epithet 'the proud' (literally 'neck‑high/lofty') functioning as the agent. Together: 'the proud one launched/harried with a night‑raid.'
segments.s669-g297: This is a nominal phrase functioning as the object of the verb in the next segment. 'همه خواسته' = 'all (the) requests/wishes', 'هرچ' (classical spelling of هر چه) = 'whatever', and 'بد' = 'ill/bad' (here qualifying 'هرچ' — 'whatever ill/complaint'). 'در حصار' places these items 'in the fortress'. Word order is noun-qualifier-place, typical of Persian poetry where the verb can follow in the next segment.
segments.s669-g298: 'نبشتند' is past 3rd-plural with the negative prefix نـ (they did not write/inscribe). It governs the following noun phrase 'چیزی که آید به کار' ('anything that would be of use'), where 'که' introduces a relative clause and 'آید' is a present/future-tense verb meaning 'come' (i.e. 'prove to be'), so 'آید به کار' = 'be of use'. The whole clause therefore negates having written anything useful.
segments.s669-g299: The verbal unit 'فرود آمد' (here split across 'فرود' and 'آمد') means 'alighted/descended/came down'. 'از دز' gives origin — 'from Dez' (the place-name Dez, not the common noun 'thief' here). 'سرافراز مرد' is an adjectival phrase 'a proud / triumphant man' (poetic epithet) describing the subject.
segments.s669-g300: 'باسب' is an adverbial compound meaning 'on horseback / by horse'. 'نبرد' is the noun 'battle' used with the motion verb 'اندر آمد' ('entered into') to mean 'entered into the battle/fray'. 'چوگرد' at the end is a poetic conjunct (چو + گرد) meaning 'as/when', linking this action to what follows or to the manner of entry.
segments.s669-g301: 'همی‌رفت' is an imperfect/continuous past form (archaic poetic) meaning 'was going / was marching'. It takes the accompaniment phrase 'با لشکر' ('with the army') and origin 'از دز' ('from Dez'), and 'به راه' specifies direction 'on the road / along the way'.
segments.s669-g302: The verb 'نکرد' (past of کردن) here is negative ('did not do'). It takes the indefinite negator 'ایچ' ('not at all / nothing') and the verbal noun 'نگاه' ('a look/glance') as its object; 'بهرام یل را' is the definite object phrase with the object marker 'را' ('at Bahram the hero'). So literally 'he did not make any look at Bahram' = 'he did not look at Bahram at all.'
segments.s669-g303: 'چوآن' = 'چو آن' is a classical subordinating phrase 'when/as that', introducing a temporal clause. 'دید' is simple past 'saw'. The idiom 'ننگ آمدش' ('shame came to him') uses آمد plus the enclitic pronoun 'ش' = 'to him', meaning 'he felt shame' or 'shame seized him'.
segments.s669-g304: وگر is used here in the sense 'although / even if'. 'چند' means 'some' or 'a few' and qualifies 'شاهی' = 'kingship / kingdom' (i.e. rule or royal power). 'بچنگ آمدش' is a compound meaning 'came into his grasp' (ب- + چنگ = into the hand/grasp; آمدش = 'came to him'). The clause expresses a concession: despite having gained some kingship, shame came to him.
segments.s669-g65: بپرموده is a past-participial/epithet meaning ‘well-instructed’ (here describing the speaker). گفت is the past tense ‘said’ (the provided lexeme list did not include the simple past sense so its sense is marked separately). ای is the vocative particle ‘O’. گریزنده is a present participle ‘fleeing’ used as an epithet, and مرد is the noun ‘man’ addressed by the speaker.
segments.s669-g66: تو is the 2nd-person singular ‘you’. گرد here functions as a verb/adverbial notion ‘to go/turn about’ (classical Persian uses گرد/گردیدن in imperatives). دلیران جنگی is a compound describing the addressees as ‘the brave (دلیران) who are warlike/fighters (جنگی)’. مگرد is a prohibitive/negative imperative form (‘do not…’) — the edition’s gloss list gives other senses for مگرد, so no exact sense index was available.
segments.s669-g67: نه مردی هنوز — a negation plus noun phrase: نه مردی = ‘not a man’ / ‘not yet manly’. هنوز‌ای is a folded expression ‘still, O…’ (هنوز + vocative ای). پسر کودکی = ‘a boy / a child’ with پسر addressing the addressee and کودکی indicating childishness or childhood.
segments.s669-g68: روا باشد is an impersonal phrase ‘it is fitting / it would be proper’. ار is the conditional ‘if’. شیرمادر is the compound ‘mother’s milk’ (i.e., being nursed), and مکی qualifies it as ‘Meccan’ (مکی = of Mecca); together the clause says the thing would be appropriate only under that condition.
segments.s669-g69: بدو گفت = ‘he said to him’ (بدو = to him/unto him). شاه‌ای is a vocative ‘O king’ or an honorific address. گراینده شیر is a descriptive noun phrase: گراینده (one who causes to incline / one who turns) + شیر (lion) — a poetic epithet addressed to the addressee.
segments.s669-g70: به خون ریختن is a noun-phrase/gerund ‘in the shedding (spilling) of blood’ (به + noun/infinitive). چند is an interrogative/adverb ‘how long / how much’. باشی is the 2nd-person subjunctive/present of بودن ‘you be / you will be’, and دلیر is the predicate adjective ‘brave’. The segment asks how long the addressee will remain brave by means of bloodshed.
segments.s669-g71: زخون سران is a genitival expression ‘from/with the blood of the chiefs’ (ز + خون سران fused in the text). سیر شد is the past ‘became sated / was satisfied’. روز جنگ ‘the day of battle’ is the temporal/agentive phrase linked to the verb; overall: ‘the day of battle was sated with the chiefs’ blood.’
segments.s669-g72: بخشکی پلنگ و بدریا نهنگ is a parallel pair: بخشکی (on the dry land / literally ‘in dryness’) modifies پلنگ ‘the leopard (on land)’, و = ‘and’, بدریا = ‘into / in the sea’, نهنگ = ‘whale’. The line uses these images to say both land and sea beasts were sated by the slaughter.
segments.s669-g257: برین is the prepositional phrase بر + این ('by/on this' or 'let this ...'). مهر و منشور یزدان is a compound noun phrase 'seal and charter of God' (مهر = seal/stamp here, منشور = charter/inscribed decree). گواست is a predicative verb/phrase meaning 'is witness' or 'bears witness' (an older poetic form). The whole segment functions as the main clause introducing what bears witness.
segments.s669-g258: که introduces a content clause 'that ...'. ما بندگانیم is a nominal sentence with the copula suffix -یم ('we are servants'); بندگانیم is 'we are servants/servants we are'. و او پادشاست joins the counterpoint 'and he is the sovereign' (پادشاه/پادشا + است contracted to پادشاست). The object marker را does not appear here; clauses are linked by که and و.
segments.s669-g259: جهانجوی را is the marked object/recipient ('to the world‑seeker' — را marks him as definite/target). نیز 'also' modifies the action. پاسخ نبشت is a past tense verb phrase 'wrote a reply' (نبشت is the older/poetic past of نوشت/نوشتن; here it means 'wrote/inscribed'). Word order: recipient + (adverb) + object + verb is acceptable in classical Persian.
segments.s669-g260: پر از آرزو نامه‌ای is a noun phrase 'a letter full of wishes' where پر از 'full of' takes a following noun phrase (آرزو). نامه‌ای is indefinite with the -ی ending 'a letter'. چون بهشت is a simile 'like paradise' modifying the letter. The overall phrase apposes/explicates the 'reply' just mentioned.
segments.s669-g261: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = به + او, 'to him'); گفت here is the speech verb 'said' (the provided sense list did not include this verbal meaning). پرموده را = 'the well‑taught one' (پرموده either an epithet or name) marked by را as direct object. با سپاه 'with an army' is an instrumental phrase modifying the verb phrase (i.e., send him with an army).
segments.s669-g262: گسی کن is an imperative 'send someone' (گسی = someone/somebody; کن = do/send, imperative of کردن or a causative). بخوبی is an adverb 'well/thoroughly'. بدین بارگاه is a locative/directional phrase 'to this court' (بدین = به + این, an archaic/poetic form 'to this'; بارگاه = court/palace). Word-by-word order shows the imperative followed by manner and destination.
segments.s669-g263: غنیمت heads the clause meaning 'as/for booty' or 'take as advantage'; که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/from which'. ازلشکرش is a fused prepositional phrase 'from his army/host' (ازل = از + ال/poetic contraction; شکرش here means 'his army/retinue'), and یافتی is past 'you obtained/you took'. The clause explains what is meant by 'غنیمت.'
segments.s669-g264: بدان بندگی is 'to that servitude/devotion' (بدان = به + آن, an older compound meaning 'to that'; بندگی = servitude/devotion). تیز بشتافتی is an exhortation 'hasten swiftly/quickly' (تیز = swift; بشتافتی = you hasten / be quick — here imperative/optative sense in context: 'hasten'). The structure is a bid: 'take X as booty, and hasten to that service.'
segments.s669-g313: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed before the verb for emphasis. یافتم is the 1st-person singular past of یافتن ('I found/obtained') and takes نامه as its direct object. زینهار here is a noun ‘warning/advertence’ modifying نامه (a “warning letter”) rather than the interjection meaning “beware.” Word order is slightly flexible/poetic: adverb + verb + object phrase.
segments.s669-g314: همی‌رفت is a poetic imperfective/past-habitual form ('would go' / 'was going') used here in combination with خواهم. خواهم (from خواهیدن) expresses the speaker's intention/future 'I will' (desire/will). Together the verbs convey the speaker's immediate resolution to go; بر + شهریار marks direction/toward the king ('to the king'). The ordering (verb + auxiliary + preposition + goal) reflects older/poetic syntax.
segments.s669-g315: مگر here introduces a concessive/conditional sense (roughly 'perhaps/if'), leading into the condition. با من ('with me') functions as the dative/benefactive phrase 'toward me'; او is the subject 'he'. چون = 'like/as' and برادر is the predicate noun 'brother.' شود is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'become' (شد used here to mark potential/becoming). Overall: 'perhaps he may become (to) me like a brother.'
segments.s669-g316: ازو is the fused archaic form of 'از او' meaning 'from him' or 'of him.' رنج here means 'harm/offense' (not merely general suffering). بر من uses بر with the sense 'against/toward' followed by من as the object 'me' (object form). سبکتر is the comparative of سبک meaning 'lighter' (in force/weight); شود again is 'become' (3rd person). Together: 'the hurt/ill from him against me will become lighter.'
segments.s669-g317: The phrase 'تو را' uses the object marker را after the pronoun for emphasis ('you'). با من ('with me') gives the scope/relationship; اکنون 'now' times the question. چه is the interrogative 'what,' and کارست is an archaic predicate (کار + -ست) meaning 'is there a matter/business' — literally 'what business is (there)?' نیز at the end means 'also/indeed' or softens the question. The whole segment forms the idiomatic question 'What business have you with me now?'
segments.s669-g318: سپردم is 1st‑person singular past 'I entrusted/handed over.' The following 'تو را' marks the recipient 'to you' (را again used emphatically with the personal pronoun). تخت شاهی is a noun phrase 'the throne of kingship' (تخت = throne, شاهی = kingship/sovereignty). و چیز 'and things' is a generalizing addition meaning 'and other matters/possessions.' The verb governs the whole object phrase: 'I entrusted to you the royal throne and other things.'
segments.s669-g145: نشستی is a past/perfect second‑person form 'you sat' (here the provided lemma list is inconsistent, so treat as the verb نشستن). بر + ین attaches to باره: برِین باره = 'on this occasion' (ین is the demonstrative/orthographic variant). The phrase 'برِ این باره' is a prepositional temporal phrase; چون زنان follows it and compares the subject to 'women' ('like women').
segments.s669-g146: پرازخون is a compound adjective meaning 'full of blood / bloodstained' modifying the coordinated nouns دل و دست ('heart and hand'); the conjunction و is fused in ودست here. The prepositional phrase بر سر زنان 'upon the heads of women' specifies the locus (literally 'on the heads of women'). Word order is poetic (adjective before nouns, prepositional phrase after).
segments.s669-g147: درباره here is an adverbial/prepositional head 'about/regarding'. بگشای is an imperative or hortative 'open / reveal / speak (of it)' (classical imperative form). و joins the next clause; زنهار is an interjection/nominal admonition 'take heed / mercy / spare (me)' used here as an object of the following verb. خواه is an imperative/verb 'ask / desire' (here instructing to 'ask' or 'beg').
segments.s669-g148: This is an imperative request phrase whose grammar is somewhat contracted in the manuscript: برشاه کشور = 'to the king of the realm' (بر + شاه forming a dative/goal phrase; کشور modifies شاه), م + را represent the object pronoun 'm(e) + را' (the tokenization here is irregular: م = 'me' and را = the object marker). یارخواه is the noun 'one who seeks a friend/ally' or 'ask for a friend' — the whole clause means 'ask the king of the realm on my behalf for a friend/ally.'
segments.s669-g149: ز is the preposition 'from'. دز گنج is a genitive/compound: depending on context either 'the thief's treasure' or a set phrase for a treasure‑store; here it functions as the source noun phrase. دینار is the object 'dinar(s)'; بیرون فرست is an imperative cluster 'send out'. The verb فرست (فرستادن) is the imperative 'send'.
segments.s669-g150: بگیتی is an adverbial/locative 'in the world / among people' placed before the verb for emphasis. نخورد here is used negatively/optatively in the sense 'may not befall / may not receive' (ن + خورد, with خورد used in the sense 'happen, befall, receive support'); آنک = 'he who / that who' introduces the relative clause. برپای بست is a perfective clause 'bound (his) feet' (برپای = 'on the feet', بست = 'tied/bound'); overall: 'may it not befall the one who tied his feet.'
segments.s669-g151: اگرگنج is a fused conditional 'اگر گنج' = 'if (there is) treasure'; classical texts often conjoin particles and nouns. داری is present indicative 'you have' (second person). ز کشور = 'from the country/realm' (ز = from). بیار is the imperative 'bring (it)'. The clause is a conditional imperative: 'If you have treasure, bring it from the land.'
segments.s669-g152: که introduces the explanatory/result clause 'for / because'. دینار is the subject 'dinar(s)'. خوارست is an archaic/uncertain predicate form in the manuscript (reading is irregular); syntactically it functions as the verb/predicate 'is ___' (the exact lexical sense is unclear in the wordlist, so treated as an uncertain predicate). برشهریار = 'for/to the king' (بر + شهریار = dative/goal). Together: 'for dinars are [of consequence / prized] by the king.'
segments.s669-g185: هنر (subject) = 'virtue/skill' placed first as topic. زیرافسوس is a fused poetic interjection (زیرا + افسوس) meaning 'alas' or 'to one's regret' inserted before the verb. پنهان شود is a passive/subjunctive verbal phrase: پنهان (past participle 'hidden') + شود (third-person singular of شدن used here as 'become / be') — together 'is/should be hidden' (classical literary present-subjunctive/passive use).
segments.s669-g186: همان دشمن = 'that same enemy' (subject). از دوست is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of a friend' meaning 'appearing among friends' or 'coming from a friend' (marks origin/disguise). خندان شود is predicate: خندان 'smiling' + شود ('becomes/comes to be') — classical phrasing meaning 'will smile' or 'is found smiling'. Word order is topical subject → origin phrase → predicate.
segments.s669-g187: دگر is a discourse marker 'another / as for the other'. آنک گفتی here functions as a relative clause 'that which you said / you mentioned' (آنک = 'that which/that who'; گفتی is the 2nd-person past verbal sense 'you said' in classical usage). شمار سپهر is a genitive construction 'the number/measure of the sky/heavens' (شمار modifying سپهر). Together: 'As for the other — that which you called the number of heaven...'.
segments.s669-g188: فزونست is an archaic fused form meaning 'is greater / exceeds' (third-person singular present). از introduces the standard comparative 'than / from' construction. تابش 'radiance / shining' is the noun being compared. هور و مهر are the two sources of light being named; together with تابش they mean 'the radiance of Hoor and Mehr'. The whole clause says that the thing named is greater than that radiance.
segments.s669-g189: ستوران و پیلان = 'herds and elephants' (two plural nouns joined). چوتخم here begins a simile: چو = classical 'when / as' and تخم/گیا (seen together with the next word) evokes 'seed / plant' — the phrase functions as a simile 'when (they were) like seeds/vegetation'. گيا (gaya) literally 'plants/vegetation' is used metaphorically to compare the beasts to seeds/grass.
segments.s669-g190: شد is the past/preterite or resultative 'became / were'. اندر دم = a locative/time phrase 'in a breath / in a moment' (اندر = 'in/within', دم = 'moment, breath'). پره آسیا = 'the blades of the mill' (پره 'blade / vane', آسیا 'mill'). The sequence says they were, in a moment, cast/ground between the mill's blades — a vivid metaphor of sudden destruction.
segments.s669-g191: بران کو is a classical topical/relative phrase 'for those (to whom)' (بران = 'for/against them' or 'upon that/for those'; کو functions like 'for whom' here). چنین = 'thus/so'. بود برگشت روز places the predicate: بود (was) + برگشت روز literally 'the turning/reversal of the day' — an idiom meaning a sudden change of fortune. Word order: topical marker → relative particle → quality → 'was the turning of the day'.
segments.s669-g192: نمانی is from ماندن 'to remain' here meaning '(you) remained' / 'were left' (classical past/resultative sense). تو هم is 'you also' (printed here as توهم but functionally the phrase 'تو هم'); شاد = 'happy/joyful'. و گیتی فروز = 'and world-illuminating' (گیتی 'the world', فروز 'bright/illuminating') — together: 'you too remained joyful and a light to the world.'
segments.s669-g121: چنین گفت is a fixed past-tense phrase meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus', گفت = past of 'say'). زان پس که is a temporal/conjunctive phrase ('زان پس' = 'after that'; که introduces the following clause). سامان جنگ is a noun phrase with سامان ('order/arrangement') governing جنگ ('of war/battle'), i.e. 'the arrangements of war.'
segments.s669-g122: کنون نیست is a present-state statement ('کنون' = now; 'نیست' = is not/does not exist), so the clause negates the existence/continuation of something. در کارکردن is a verbal-noun expression ('در' = in; 'کارکردن' = to act/do) marking the sphere of action. درنگ ('delay, tarrying') is the negated element: 'there is no delay in acting' (i.e. do not delay).
segments.s669-g123: یلان سینه راگفت is a poetic address: یلان ('heroes') + سینه ('bosom/heart') gives the epithet 'the brave-hearted'; راگفت (را + گفت) is a finite past verb 'he said/told' with the object marker را attached to the verb form, indicating he 'spoke to/ordered' them. تا سه هزار uses تا to indicate the intended number/extent ('to/amounting to three thousand').
segments.s669-g124: ازان جنگیان برگزیند سوار is the complement of the command: ازان ('of those/from those') introduces the source group; جنگیان ('the warriors/belligerents') names that group; برگزیند is a present/subjunctive-like or jussive verb form meaning 'choose/select' (here expressing what is to be done), and سوار ('rider/horseman') is the noun selected — 'choose three thousand riders from those warriors.'
segments.s669-g125: چهار از یلان نیز آذرگشسب lists persons to be mounted: چهار از یلان ('four of the heroes') with نیز ('also/likewise') adding them to the previous pick, and آذرگشسب is the proper name placed appositionally or as one of those four. Word order is emphatic/poetic: numeral + از + group + نیز + name.
segments.s669-g126: ازان جنگیان برنشاند بر اسب is the action clause: ازان جنگیان ('from those warriors') names the source; برنشاند is a past/transitive verb here meaning 'he caused to be seated/placed' (i.e. 'set on'); بر + اسب ('on a horse') is the prepositional phrase showing where they were seated. The verb governs the object implied earlier (the four heroes).
segments.s669-g127: بفرمود تا هر که را یافتند begins with an honorific/polite past verb بفرمود ('he ordered/said' — a courtly past form). تا introduces the subordinate clause of command/purpose ('that'), هر که را یافتند is a free relative ('whomever/whoever they found') with هر که = 'whoever' and را marking the direct object of یافتند ('they found').
segments.s669-g128: بگردن زدن تیز بشتافتند is the result/action they were ordered to take: بگردن ('around the neck') plus زدن ('to strike') forms the verbal phrase 'to strike at the neck' (i.e. kill/behead). تیز ('swift/quick') modifies the verb, and بشتافتند is a past plural intransitive 'they hurried/ hastened' describing that they quickly carried out the action.
segments.s669-g201: نیایم is a negative-present/subjunctive form meaning “I do not / I will not come” (نیا + ـم with negative sense). پیش تو = “before you / in your presence”; همان and ناگهان are adverbial modifiers meaning “just / exactly” and “suddenly,” so the whole chunk means “I will not suddenly appear before you.” Word order puts the negated verb first for emphasis, then the place phrase and adverbs.
segments.s669-g202: بترسم is a first‑person verb meaning “I fear” (present/subjunctive nuance). که introduces a subordinate clause (“that / for”), and برمن سرآید is an idiomatic causative/impersonal sequence: سرآید (to come/arrive) with the prepositional phrase بر من = “upon me” yields “that (a) time comes upon me” (i.e. my time/doom arrives). زمان is the noun “time/moment.”
segments.s669-g203: The pair یکی ... یکی is a classical Persian distributive/contrastive formula meaning “at one time ... at another ...”. بنده‌ای uses the indefinite/humble form of بنده to mean “a humble servant” (humble self‑reference). The clause simply juxtaposes two states: “at one time I (am) a servant, at another (I am) a shah.” The personal pronoun من is explicit for emphasis.
segments.s669-g204: بربنده is a prepositional phrase (بر + بنده) meaning “in the presence of / toward the servant.” کی شوم is a rhetorical/questioning use of کی “when / ever” plus شوم “(I) become” (subjunctive/optative flavour) — here functioning as “when (would I) become …?” زار و خوار are two adjectives in a short coordinate phrase meaning “wretched and humiliated/despicable.” The whole asks rhetorically how the speaker could be degraded before his servant.
segments.s669-g205: به جنگ‌ت = “to/for your war/battle” (به + جنگ + enclitic ـت “your”). نیایم again expresses “I will not come/go”; همان بی‌سپاه means “just/straightforwardly without an army” or “unescorted.” The phrase denies the speaker will enter the fight alone.
segments.s669-g206: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause “for/because.” خواند is a past third‑person verb here meaning “called” (sense: to call/name). دیوانه is the predicate noun “madman/mad.” مرا = “me” is the object (in the provided segmentation it appears split as م + را; grammatically this is the object pronoun 'm' + object marker را). نیکخواه (‘well‑wisher’) is the agent placed after the object by inversion — the clause means “because the well‑wisher called me mad.”
segments.s669-g207: اگر خواهم begins a conditional clause: اگر “if” + خواهم “I desire / I should ask” (present/future sense). از شاه تو = “from your king.” زینهار is an interjection meaning “beware / take heed” that follows here as a warning attached to the conditional; it can be read as the speaker’s aside or as a warning addressed to the addressee.
segments.s669-g208: چوتنگی is a fused archaic/poetic unit (چو + تنگی) meaning “when narrowness/straiteness (i.e. poverty)…”; بَروی آیدم is an idiomatic sequence corresponding to بر من آید “comes upon me” (آیدم = “it comes to me / befalls me”). نیست عار is a fixed negation phrase “it is not a shame/disgrace.” Together: “if hardship befalls me, it is no disgrace.”
segments.s669-g81: ز مادر (from mother) is a prepositional phrase placed before the verb for emphasis; همه (everyone) is the subject; مرگ را uses the definite/object marker را after مرگ to mark 'death' as the destined condition; زاده‌ایم is the perfect/pluperfect passive-like form 'we have been born' (here translated as 'we are born to/into'), so the whole clause literally reads 'From mother, everyone we have been born to death.'
segments.s669-g82: This line uses an archaic correlative particle ar/ار twice to set up an either...or contrast; ای دون is a vocative/insult 'O base/ignoble one' (address or characterization); که introduces the following predicate clause; ترکیم here means 'we are Turks / we are of that stock' or more generally 'we are lowly' (the available lemma senses don't list 'Turk', so the exact ethnic reading is context-dependent); the second ار functions as the 'or' of the correlative pair, and آزاده‌ایم is the present 'we are free/noble-hearted'. The clause thus contrasts being lowly vs. being noble.
segments.s669-g83: گریزانم is an adjectival/predicative form plus the 1st‑person clitic 'م' meaning 'I am fleeing/avoiding' (here 'I am on the run'); و links clauses; تو is the (emphatic) subject 'you'; پس 'then/after' is adverbial; اندر دمان is a locative phrase 'in/at the hem' (دِمان = skirt/hem) placed after the subject for emphasis. Word order is somewhat inverted: the full negative verb appears in the next segment, so this segment sets up 'I flee, and you, then, (in) at the hem...'.
segments.s669-g84: نیابی is the negative present/subjunctive form 'you do not / will not find' (older Shahnameh usage for a future/expectation); مرا is 'me' realized here as two pieces (م + را) where را is the definite/object marker attached to the object; تا introduces the temporal clause 'until'; نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive 'it does not come' and زمان means 'time/moment'. Together: 'you will not find me until the time comes.'
segments.s669-g85: اگر starts a conditional 'if'; باز گردم is a subjunctive/optative 'I return again' (گردم here = 'I turn/come back'); سلیحی (سلیح + ی) is 'a weapon' (object) with the adjectival suffix that often appears in older Persian; بچنگ is a contraction of به چنگ 'into the grip' meaning 'to seize' or 'with (weapon) in hand' — the whole clause: 'If I return again and seize a weapon...'.
segments.s669-g86: مگر here is a rhetorical particle ('wouldn't I/indeed would I not') rather than a strict negative-except; من is the pronoun 'I'; شوم is the present subjunctive 'may I become / might I be made'; کشته‌گر is an agent noun 'slayer/killer'; تو is 'you'; به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in battle' (به = in/at, جنگ = battle). The clause expresses a rhetorical question: 'would I not become your slayer in battle?'
segments.s669-g87: مکن is the prohibitive 'do not'; تیز مغزی is a compound 'sharp of mind' (تیز = sharp, مغزی = pertaining to the brain/mind) meaning 'clever/keen' or here admonishing sharp cunning; و links the qualities; آتش سری literally 'fire-headed' (آتش = fire, سری = head) meaning 'hot‑headed' or 'fiery-tempered'. The speaker is warning 'do not be clever/sharp and fiery-headed'.
segments.s669-g88: نه is the negation 'not'; زین سان is an archaic deictic expression 'in this manner/so' (زین = this/archaic deictic, سان = like/as); بود is past 'was'; مهتر لشکری is the nominal phrase 'the chief/leader of the army' (مهتر = chief, لشکری = of the soldiery). The clause states that a proper army-chief was not of this sort.
segments.s669-g289: "ز گردان برآمد یکی آفرین" is an inverted noun phrase: ز (from) introduces the source, گردان (the ranks/troop) is the source, and برآمد (arose) is the past‑tense verb placed before the exclamation phrase. یکی آفرین = "one/acclamation" (a single shout) with the numeral/article-like یکی placed before the noun in archaic/poetic word order; آفرین here is a noun (an acclamation), not the verbal stem.
segments.s669-g290: "که گفتی بجنبید روی زمین" is a reported‑speech clause introduced by که (that/who/which) linking the acclamation to its words. گفتی is used here as the speech or utterance (a nominal/poetic use) introducing the quotation; بجنبید is a 2nd‑person plural imperative/subjunctive meaning “move!”; روی زمین is a prepositional phrase (روی = upon/on, زمین = the earth/ground) specifying where to move. The clause thus reports the content of the acclamation.
segments.s669-g291: همان نامور نامه زینهار is a compact noun phrase: همان (that/the very) as a demonstrative, نامور (renowned) as adjectival epithet, نامه (letter) the head noun, and زینهار functioning as a postpositive epithet meaning "warning/advertence." Together: "that renowned letter of warning."
segments.s669-g292: که introduces a relative clause describing the letter. پرموده را is an element preceding the verb and is marked by the object/topic marker را (an instance of poetic/inverted word order): پرموده (literally "well‑taught/experienced") is here the person or recipient related to the action. آمد is the past tense "came/arrived" and از شهریار specifies the source "from the king." In short: "which came from the king to/for the experienced one."
segments.s669-g293: بدان here functions as the compound prepositional phrase be+ān = "to that/unto that" (classical contraction often written separately). دز (thief) is the noun involved and فرستاد is the past‑tense verb "sent." نزدیک اوی is a locative/adverbial phrase "near him." Word order is typical of older Persian: prepositional phrase + object + verb + local modifier.
