segments.s669-g294: درخشنده شد is a predicative verbloc: درخشنده (radiant/shining) + شد (became) — "became radiant." جان تاریک اوی is the noun phrase whose state changes: جان (soul/life) modified by تاریک (dark) with the pronoun اوی (his/he) attached. So: "his dark soul became radiant."
segments.s669-g295: فرود آمد is a two‑word verbal phrase: فرود (alighting/descent) + آمد (came) = "alighted/descended." از introduces the source or context "from," باره is a noun here meaning "occasion/instance," and نامدار postpositively modifies it as "renowned/famous." The phrase describes the act of coming down or alighting in relation to a famed circumstance.
segments.s669-g296: بسی is an adverbial/intensifying word meaning "much/very/abundantly." آفرین is the substantive "acclamation/praise" (here plural/collective in sense), and خواند is the past tense verb "he recited/sang/called" — i.e., he voiced the acclamations. برشهریار is a prepositional phrase (بر + شهریار) meaning "to/upon the king." So the whole clause: "he loudly/abundantly cried out praises to the king."
segments.s669-g177: This is a compact, somewhat inverted poetic clause: دروغ (subject) + آزمایست (predicate ‘is a test’) with the agent/source چرخ بلند placed after the verb phrase. آزمایست is an older/poetic 3rd-person copula/verb meaning ‘is a test’ (آزمایش + است condensed). چرخ بلند literally ‘the high wheel/sky’ functions as the agent/source: ‘tested by the lofty sky’ (typical classical Persian allows such inversion and agent following the predicate).
segments.s669-g178: The core structure is تودل را ... مبند: تودل را is the definite object (the inmost heart), marked by را; بگستاخی is an adverbial/instrumental phrase ‘in insolence/with boldness’ that describes the manner; مبند is a negative imperative (م‑ prefix + بند from بستن) meaning ‘do not close’ or ‘do not shut’. Overall: ‘Do not shut your inmost heart in insolence.’
segments.s669-g179: پدرم is the noun with the first‑person possessive suffix (‘my father’). آن is the demonstrative modifying the following adjectives; دلیر جهاندیده are attributive adjectives placed before the head noun (typical Persian adjective order) and مرد is the head noun ‘man/hero’. The phrase is a nominal apposition: ‘My father, that brave, worldly‑wise man.’
segments.s669-g180: که introduces a relative clause modifying مرد (‘who/whom’). دیدی is 2nd‑person past ‘you saw’. ورا is the object pronoun (‘him’). روزگار here is the agent ‘Fate/time’ and is placed before the verb in classical word order. نبرد in this context functions as a past verbal idea meaning ‘did not seize/overcome’ (poetic/archaic usage) rather than the plain noun ‘battle’; the clause reads ‘whom, when you saw him, Fate did not overcome.’
segments.s669-g181: This clause uses a sequence of genitive and dative relations: زمین (subject/‘earth’) followed by سم اسب (‘the horse’s hoof’ – the possessed noun cluster) and ورا as the beneficiary/dative ‘to him’. بنده بود is a predicative phrase ‘was a servant/slave’ (بنده as predicative noun + بود past copula), meaning ‘the earth served him’ or ‘the earth was subject to his horse’s hoof.’
segments.s669-g182: برایش is the preposition برای ‘for’ with the enclitic possessive/pronominal ش (for him). فلک نیز پوینده بود is the predicate: فلک ‘the heavens/sky’, نیز ‘also’, پوینده an adjectival/participle form meaning ‘moving’ or ‘in motion’, and بود the past copula. Together: ‘the heavens, too, were moving for him’ (i.e. the heavens followed/served him).
segments.s669-g183: بجست is a past tense verb meaning ‘he sought’ (here used as the main verb). آنک is a relative/connector ‘that which/what’. اورا is the object pronoun ‘it/him’ (referring back to the thing sought). نبایست + جست is a modal construction where نبایست (negative of بایست in the sense ‘ought/should’) with the verb جست (‘to seek’) expresses ‘ought not to be sought’ or ‘should not have been sought’ — classical/poetic modality where the infinitive/root follows the modal.
segments.s669-g184: بپیچید is a past form meaning ‘he turned aside’ or ‘he erred/turned’. ز is the poetic form of از (‘from’/‘because of’) and takes the following noun in the ablative/causal sense; اندیشه نادرست is the nominal phrase ‘wrong thought/thinking’. So the clause reads literally ‘he turned away from (or because of) wrong thought.’
segments.s669-g57: ز introduces a cause or source ('from/because of') and precedes the noun phrase; 'ترکان جنگی' is a noun plus an epithet meaning 'Turkish fighters' (جنگی as attributive). فراوان follows the noun phrase and here qualifies the quantity; نماند is a 3rd-person past negative/result verb 'remained/not remained' so the whole clause means 'not many of the Turkish fighters remained.'
segments.s669-g58: ز خون is a prepositional/cause phrase 'from/by the blood'; سنگها is the subject ('stones' or, in poetic context, 'gems'). جز به مرجان نماند is the predicate: جز 'except', به here marks the resulting form/into-ness ('as/into'), مرجان is the resultant noun, and نماند again 'remained/not remained' — together: 'of the gems, because of the blood nothing remained except (turned into) coral.'
segments.s669-g59: گریزان is a present/ongoing participial adjective 'fleeing' placed before the verb; همی‌رفت is the habitual/continuous past 'was going/kept going.' مهتر is the subject 'the chief.' چو + گرد is a simile 'like dust' (چو = 'as/like', گرد = 'dust'), so 'the fleeing chief kept moving like dust.'
segments.s669-g60: This clause supplies the chief's physical state. دهن (mouth) followed by the adjective خشک (dry) forms 'mouth [was] dry' with the copula omitted; و links the next phrase. لبها (lips) + شده (past participle 'become') + لاجورد (lapis/livid blue) yields 'his lips had become lapis (blue).' The subject (he/the chief) is omitted and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s669-g61: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so.' تا سپیده‌دمان is a temporal phrase 'until dawn/daybreak.' بردمید is a past tense verb (classical/poetic) meaning 'carried on / carried off / endured' in context — here idiomatically 'continued/seemed to hold on until dawn.' The clause gives the time-span: 'thus until daybreak it continued.'
segments.s669-g62: شب تیره‌گون is a compound adjectival phrase 'the dark-colored (gloomy) night' (تیره + گون forming 'darkish'). دامن ('hem/skirt') is the possessed noun; اندر کشید is a verbal phrase 'drew in' or 'pulled inward' (اندر = 'in/within', کشید = 'drew/pulled' — here the night withdrawing its skirts is a poetic way to say night receded).
segments.s669-g63: سپهدار ایران is the title + genitive 'the commander of Iran' and serves as subject. بترکان is the prepositional phrase 'to/against the Turks' (classical contracted form of به ترکان); رسید is the perfect/past verb 'arrived/reached.' Word order is subject + (to/against) + verb: 'the commander of Iran reached the Turks.'
segments.s669-g64: خروشی is the noun 'a roar/cry' and is what follows as the direct action; چوشیر is a poetic contraction of چو شیر 'like a lion' (a simile modifying the roar). ژیان ('life' / 'the living') here intensifies the cry or its effect (poetic: 'of life' / 'that stirs the living'). برکشید is the past verb 'raised/lifted' (he raised a roar). Together: 'he raised a lion-like roar.'
segments.s669-g217: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral/article 'one/a certain' placed before the coordinated nouns مهر و منشور. مهر is the noun 'seal' and is coordinated with منشور 'charter'. باید is the modal 'must/ought to' placed after the noun phrase to express necessity; همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (classical Persian) adding emphasis ('indeed/just').
segments.s669-g218: بدین here is composed of the prepositional prefix ب- plus the following element and functions as 'with/by this'; مژده is the noun 'good news/tidings'. بر introduces the beneficiary/location and with سور ('banquet/feast') yields 'for/at the banquet'. باید and همی repeat the necessity + emphatic pattern from the previous clause, creating a parallel construction.
segments.s669-g219: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'so that'). خاقان is the subject 'khagan'. زما is an oblique/genitive element meaning 'by us' or 'from us' (agent in a passive-style construction) placed after the subject. زینهاری is the noun 'warning', and شود is the classical present/subjunctive of شدن used to mean 'become / be made' (here roughly 'be warned' / 'may be warned').
segments.s669-g220: ازان is an adverbial/prepositional 'from that' linking this clause to the previous one; برتری is the subject noun 'superiority/preeminence'. سوی is a preposition 'toward/to' indicating direction or change of state, taking the noun خواری 'humiliation' as its complement. شود again is 'become' (intransitive) indicating transformation ('preeminence becomes humiliation').
segments.s669-g221: چونامه 'letter/epistle' is the subject; بیامد is the past tense 'came' (classical form). به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' (literally 'to the king's vicinity' or 'to the king's presence'), with به marking direction toward the noun نزدیک acting adverbially.
segments.s669-g222: بابر is a personal name (here used as the agent); اندر آورد is the verbal phrase 'brought in' where اندر is a locative/adverbial 'in/into' and آورد is the past tense 'brought'. فرخ qualifies کلاه: an epithet meaning 'fortunate/bright/splendid', so فرخ کلاه = 'the splendid/ornamented hat.'
segments.s669-g223: فرستاد is the past tense 'sent' (here with the sense 'sent for' / 'sent to fetch'); و links clauses. ایرانیان is the direct object or the group affected 'the Iranians', and رابخواند (را بخواند) in context functions as 'read to them' / 'he read (it) aloud' — بخواند is the past tense 'read/recited' with the object marker را attached.
segments.s669-g224: برنامور here is an epithet/adjective meaning 'illustrious/renowned' (functioning as the person placed on the throne). تخت شاهی is a nominal phrase 'the royal/kingship throne' with تخت 'throne' and شاهی as an adjectival/genitive 'of kingship' or 'royal'. نشاند is an archaic transitive past meaning 'caused to sit / seated / set (on the throne)'. The verb takes برنامور as its object (the one seated) and تخت شاهی as the seat/place.
segments.s669-g161: وگر is the conditional 'if' (classical/poetic). جنگ را is marked with the object/definite marker را; here the phrase (literally 'the war-RA') is tied to یار داری کسی — the structure means 'if you have someone as a companion in war' (یار دارَی = 'you have a companion'). The word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: subordinate conditional + noun phrase + main verb 'داری'.
segments.s669-g162: همان here functions like 'then/that (being so)' linking to the conditional; گنج و دینار داری بسی is a simple predicate: داری (present simple 'you have') takes the coordinated direct objects 'treasure and dinars', and بسی is a postpositive adverb meaning 'enough/plenty'. The clause states the result of the condition.
segments.s669-g163: بزن is an imperative meaning 'strike/play' here used with a musical instrument (کوس, the war-drum), so it functions as 'beat the drum'. The coordinating و links this command to the following clause; این کینها بازخواه uses بازخواه as a future/verb-like form 'will return' (poetic), so the whole clause reads 'beat the drum and these hatreds/hostilities will return.'
segments.s669-g164: بود + خواسته functions as a conditional/counterfactual idea ('were there a wish' or 'had he wished'); classical Persian often uses بود with a noun/participle to mean 'if there were ...'. تنگ ناید سپاه is a compact clause: تنگ 'narrow/scant' and ناید here 'does not come' (poetic present/future sense) with سپاه 'army' as the subject — 'the army would not come (be) constrained/scant.'
segments.s669-g165: چوآمد is the fused classical form meaning 'when (he) came' (چو + آمد). فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger'; داد 'gave' is the main verb; این پیام 'this message' is the direct object. Word order is compressed and typical of epic diction: temporal subordinate (چوآمد) + subject + verb + object.
segments.s669-g166: چو بشنید (written here as one token چوبشنید) = 'when he heard' (temporal clause). ز (poetic form of از) means 'from/of it' (i.e. 'on hearing it'). The coordinating/connecting و (here functioning like 'then/so') links to the following noun phrase مرد جوینده کام — 'the man (مرد) described by the participial/attributive noun جوینده (seeker) of کام (fulfillment/desire)'.
segments.s669-g167: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic order meaning 'he gave such an answer / he answered thus' (چنین 'thus', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). The clause که او را بگوی uses که as a complementizer introducing reported speech or instruction; او را is the object phrase 'to him' (را marks the object/recipient), and بگوی is the present-subjunctive/imperative form of گفتن used to mean 'tell' or 'say to him' (here: 'tell him').
segments.s669-g168: که راز جهان introduces the content: 'that the secret of the world ...'. تا توانی is an extent clause meaning 'as far as you are able' (تا = 'as far as/insofar as', توانی = 'you can' in present subjunctive/modal sense). مجوی is the negative prohibitive form 'do not seek' (classical imperative/subjunctive with negation), so the whole clause says 'do not seek the secret of the world as far as you can.'
segments.s669-g265: Imperative clause with destination fronted: بدرگه (to the court) names the destination, then فرست is the imperative 'send'. آنچ اندر خورست is a relative/nominal phrase 'that which is inside' (آنچ = 'that which', اندر = 'in/within') with خورست functioning as the predicate describing what is inside. The whole means 'Send to the court whatever is within.'
segments.s669-g266: A short verbless clause idiomatically rendered as 'X is your helper': تو را is topical/benefactive ('for you' or 'to you' with the emphatic/object marker را), کردگار جهان is the noun phrase 'the Creator of the world', and یاورست is the predicative (ياور + -ست, an archaic/poetic copula form = 'is a helper'). The order places the beneficiary first for emphasis.
segments.s669-g267: Imperative: نگه کن = 'look/watch!' (نگه + کن, imperative). بجایی here points to the spot/position ('to the place' / 'at the proper spot') and که introduces a relative clause 'where/that' followed by دشمن بود ('the enemy was'). So: 'Watch the place where the enemy was.'
segments.s669-g268: Conditional continuation introduced by وگر ('and if / but if'). دشمنی را is topical/emphatic ('as for the enmity/enemy'), نشیمن is a noun 'seat/dwelling/position' and بود is the copula 'was'. Together: 'and if there was an enemy seat/stronghold.' The particle را marks دشمنی as the topic/thing to be considered.
segments.s669-g269: Series of imperatives. بگیر = 'take/seize!'. ونگه (و + نگه) = conjunction + 'look/keep watch' and together with دار forms the verb phrase 'نگهدار' (keep/watch over), here expressed as two pieces; خانش is 'his khan/inn' (خان + -ش 'his') and بسوز is the imperative 'burn!'. In short: 'Seize it, keep watch, and burn his dwelling/inn.' Note: دار here participates in the verbal compound نگه‌دار (keep), so its dictionary senses as a noun do not match this verbal use.
segments.s669-g270: A short benedictory/adverbial phrase: به فرخ پی وفال = 'for (or to) good fortune and omen' (به = 'to/for', فرخ = 'fortunate/lucky', پی = 'following/after' used here in the sense 'in connection with' and وفال = 'fortune/omen'), and گیتی فروز is an attributive compound meaning 'world-brightening' or 'may the world be bright' (فروز used adjectivally/verblike to mean 'illuminate/glorify'). The phrase wishes that the world be brightened by good omen.
segments.s669-g271: Conditional opening with a vocative: گر ('if') introduces the condition; ای دون is the address 'O base/ignoble one' (ای as vocative + دون 'low/ignoble'); که introduces the content clause 'that/which' — لشکر فزون بایدت = 'you need an abundant/more numerous army' where بایدت is باید + enclitic ت 'you' meaning 'you must/have need of'.
segments.s669-g272: Result clause: فزونتر بود is a comparative 'an even greater/more numerous (force) there will be' (فزونتر = more abundant, بود = 'was/will be' used here in a poetic narrative sense), and رنج بگزایدت is a predication 'it will cause you suffering' (رنج = 'suffering', بگزایدت is an optative/causative-like verbal form meaning 'may it cause/appoint you' or 'it will bring you'). Together: 'Even more will come and will cause you suffering.'
segments.s669-g97: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/away (از)'; here it introduces what is to be driven off. سرکینه is treated as a proper name (the direct object), and وجنگ is the conjunctional form (و+جنگ) meaning 'and war' attached to it. را is the definite-object marker marking the whole coordinated object, and دورکن is an imperative compound 'دور کن' = 'drive away/banish' addressed to an implicit agent.
segments.s669-g98: بخوبی is an adverb 'well/thoroughly' modifying the following action. منش here means 'disposition/manners' (i.e. the way the celebration should be done). بریکی is an adjective 'delicate/refined' qualifying منش (style). سورکن is the verbal phrase 'سور کن' = 'make a feast/celebrate' (imperative), so the whole cluster issues a second exhortation: 'celebrate well, in a refined manner.'
segments.s669-g99: چو بشنید (here written as چوبشنید) is a temporal clause 'when/on hearing'. بهرام is the subject. The sequence ز و (ز + و) functions here as a poetic compressed prepositional/temporal marker ('from/thereon' or 'and so'), and بازگشت is the past verb 'returned' (he returned). The clause thus reads: 'When Bahram heard, he returned.'
segments.s669-g100: که introduces the following result/relative clause ('and so/that'), برساز is the stem 'بر ساز' = 'to make/establish' used with گشت to indicate coming into a state of having established something. شاهی is the noun 'kingship/royalty' as the object of برساز, خوش‌آواز is an adjective 'pleasant‑sounding / of good reputation' modifying شاهی, and گشت is the past copula/verb 'became'. The construction conveys: 'and so he became (one who) established a kingship of good renown.'
segments.s669-g101: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از جنگ is the prepositional phrase 'from war'. آن لشکر is 'that army/host' (آن as demonstrative), آسوده is a predicate adjective 'at peace/relieved', and شد is the past copular 'became/was'. Together: 'When that host was relieved from war...' (literally: 'when from war that host became relieved').
segments.s669-g102: بلشکر is the prepositional/ablative phrase formed with the prefix ب- ('with / in the company of an army'). گه is a temporal/occasional adverb ('then/at that time'). شاه is the subject 'the king', پرموده is an adjectival predicate meaning 'seasoned/experienced', and شد is the copula 'became'. The clause means roughly 'then, with the army, the king became experienced.'
segments.s669-g103: همی‌گشت is an iterative/continuous past tense 'kept going / went about' (همی- marks ongoing action). بر گرد دشت نبرد is a compound prepositional phrase 'around the plain of battle' (بر گرد = 'around', دشت 'plain', نبرد 'battle' specifying the plain). Word order is typical poetic Persian with modifiers following.
segments.s669-g104: سرسرکشان is the plural noun (collective) 'the proud/headstrong ones'. را is the object marker attaching to that noun. زتن literally 'from the body' (از + تن) is an idiom meaning to separate one from his body (i.e. to slay). دورکرد is a transitive past verb 'removed/put away' (here meaning 'separated/killed'). The phrase therefore means 'he separated the proud from their bodies' (he killed the headstrong).
segments.s669-g169: The sequence places the pronoun 'تو' (you) up front, then the predicate 'گستاخ گشتی' (became insolent) — a typical Subject + predicate order. 'بگیتی' is an adverbial phrase 'in the world' modifying the verb, and 'مگر' introduces a contrastive subordinate clause meaning 'unless' or 'except that'.
segments.s669-g170: 'که' here begins the subordinate clause introduced by 'مگر' ('unless that...'). 'رنج نخستینت آمد' is literal 'your first suffering came' (simple past). The final word 'ببر' is the imperative sense here ('take!') used poetically in a compressed clause meaning 'has taken you' or 'take you' — a jussive/urgent flavor rather than the noun 'tiger'.
segments.s669-g171: 'به پیروزی اندر تو' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'with regard to victory, in you' (i.e. 'victory within you'). The verb group 'کشی مکن' is a negative directive: 'مکن' = 'do not', and 'کشی' (here best read as 'to cultivate/bring about') is the action being negated — 'do not cultivate/cherish (victory) inside you.'
segments.s669-g172: A straightforward conditional: 'اگر تو نوی هست' = 'if you are new' (اگر + copula). The final pair 'گیتی کهن' is a short pithy statement appended after the condition: 'the world is old.' Word order is normal for classical/poetic Persian (predicate can appear after the condition).
segments.s669-g173: Negative verb + indefinite 'نداند کسی' functions as 'no one knows' (classical negative construction). 'راز گردان سپهر' is a genitive/attributive group 'the secret of the revolving/turning sky' with 'گردان' qualifying 'سپهر'.
segments.s669-g174: 'نه هرگز' is emphatic negation 'never at all'; 'نماید' (3rd-singular) = 'shows' or 'reveals'; 'بما' = 'to us'; 'نیز' = 'also/too' (here 'either'); and 'چهر' = 'face/appearance'. The whole line says the heavens never disclose their face to us.
segments.s669-g175: 'زمهتر' is an obscure/archaic noun here (an old word or name used by the poet) serving as the topic; 'نه خوبست' = 'is not good'; and 'کردن فسوس' is an infinitival phrase 'to do/utter فسوس' where 'فسوس' means 'insidious whisper/idle talk'. The whole segment condemns that kind of whispering or behavior.
segments.s669-g176: The construction 'مرا ... بود' expresses possession/existence for the speaker: literally 'to me was ...' = 'I had ...'. 'هم سپه بود' = 'I also had an army', and the following 'و هم پیل وکوس' lists additional items 'and also elephants and drums' (piling nouns with 'هم' and 'و'). 'بود' is the past copula here.
segments.s669-g113: بگفت is the perfect/past reporting verb 'he said.' آنک is a classical relative/demonstrative introducing the reported clause ('that/which' or 'who'). ما را چه آمد is the idiom 'what came to us' meaning 'what befell us' (ما را = the experiencer marked by را; چه آمد = 'what happened'). بروی here connects the verb phrase to the following source phrase (see next segment); the whole segment functions as the reported question/complaint.
segments.s669-g114: ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' ترکان is the plural noun 'the Turks.' و links the two coordinated sources. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying شاه 'king.' پرخاشجوی is a post‑nominal adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' describing that king. Together this segment supplies the source/agent of the misfortune named in the previous clause.
segments.s669-g115: که here introduces an explanatory/causal clause ('because'). از بیم تیغ او is a causal prepositional phrase 'from/for fear of his sword' (از = 'from/because of'; بیم = 'fear'; تیغ او = 'his sword'). سوی چاره شد is a verbal phrase: سوی = 'toward', چاره = 'a remedy/means', شد = past 'became/turned' — 'they turned toward/ sought a remedy.'
segments.s669-g116: وزان = 'and/from that' links result to the preceding clause. جایگه (place / position / rank) is the thing affected; شد is 'became' ('their place became ...'). خوار = 'despised, humiliated' is a predicate adjective. و introduces the next coordinated result: آواره شد = 'became displaced/wandering' (آواره = 'displaced, homeless'; شد = 'became').
segments.s669-g117: وزین روی is a classical descriptive phrase 'with a grave/dignified face' (وزین = 'dignified/weighty', روی = 'face/countenance') modifying the subject. خاقان is the subject 'the Khaghan.' در دز is locative 'at/in Dez' (دز here is the place-name). ببست is the past verb 'he closed/shut' (transitive: he shut the gate).
segments.s669-g118: بانبوه is an adverbial phrase 'with many / in great numbers' (it modifies the following action). و simply coordinates. اندیشه اندر نشست is idiomatic: اندیشه 'thought/intention', اندر 'in/within', نشست 'sat' used here to mean 'he settled into thought' or 'took counsel / pondered.' The subject (Khaghan) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s669-g119: بگشتند is the past plural 'they returned/turned back.' گرد here means 'around/about' (or 'around the place'); در دز = 'in/at Dez' (location). بسی modifies the motion as 'in many numbers / in abundance.' The whole segment reports the movement of troops/people back into the fortress/place.
segments.s669-g120: نانست (here spelled ندانست) is the past of دانستن with negative prefix 'ن-' meaning 'did not know.' The construction ندانست ... کسی renders 'no one knew' (کسی functioning as the indefinite/negative subject). سامان جنگش = 'the state/arrangement of his war/troops' (سامان = order/condition; جنگش = 'his war/army'). Together: 'no one knew the condition of his army.'
segments.s669-g249: فرستاده را نیز دینار داد is a simple past clause with the verb داد at the end (SOV order). فرستاده را uses the object marker را to mark the messenger as the indirect/direct object; نیز is an adverb meaning “also,” and دینار is the direct object (what was given). The subject (the king/actor) is omitted and understood from the context.
segments.s669-g250: یکی بدره و چیز بسیار داد continues the same past action with داد at the end. یکی = “one” (a single item); بدره is a poetic noun ‘moon/full‑moon’ used here as an image for a lustrous jewel (a moonlike ornament); چیز بسیار = “many things” (چیز = thing, بسیار qualifying it as many). The clause lists additional gifts; again the subject is omitted.
segments.s669-g251: چو introduces a temporal clause here meaning “when.” خلعت is the robe of honour being given; the sequence ب + دان + مرد دانا in the manuscript represents بېدان / بدان meaning “to that” (ب = to; دان here functions as an old/dialectal demonstrative forming بدان = “to that”). مرد دانا = “that/ the wise man.” سپرد is the past tense verb ‘entrusted/handed over’ placed at the end in SOV order. (Note: دان as listed in the word‑list has unrelated senses; here it is functioning as part of the demonstrative 'بدان'.)
segments.s669-g252: ورا is the clitic pronoun meaning “to him” (marking the person receiving a designation). مهتر پهلوانان is a nominal phrase ‘chief of the champions’ (مهتر = chief/head among, پهلوانان = champions). شمرد is the past of شماریدن used here in the sense “to reckon/consider/declare” (he counted/declared him to be). Word order: object/pronoun before the predicate, verb final.
segments.s669-g253: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense verb often used for a sovereign meaning ‘he commanded/said.’ پس = then/next; تا + verb here introduces the time clause ‘when/until the scribe came’ (تا بیامد دبیر = until/when the scribe arrived). Note the subordinate clause structure: تا + verb (بیامد) + subject (دبیر).
segments.s669-g254: نبشتند is the past plural ‘they wrote’ (verb first here). ز + و in the manuscript is the poetic contraction زو = “from him” (ز = from, و representing the old clitic for ‘him’); together they mark the origin/authority of the letter. نامه‌ای is an indefinite direct object ‘a letter.’ بر حریر is a locative phrase ‘on (silk)’ specifying the material written upon. Word order here: verb – (from‑him) – object – locative.
segments.s669-g255: که introduces a relative clause referring back to the letter: “which.” پرموده is an adjectival/predicative participial form meaning ‘well‑composed / well‑prepared’ (here describing the letter). خاقان names the author/authority (the Khagan). چویار is a fused expression meaning ‘as a friend’ or ‘in the manner of a friend,’ and منست (from منس) is a predicative adjective ‘is fitting/appropriate.’ Together: “which, well composed by the Khagan, is fitting as a friend’s [message].”
segments.s669-g256: بهرمزد is a prepositional phrase here meaning ‘for reward / as a recompense’ (literally ‘for reward’). در introduces the frame ‘in’ or ‘as (regards)’, زینهار is a noun ‘warning / admonition,’ and منست again makes the predicate ‘is fitting/appropriate.’ The clause says that, as regards reward, the letter functions as an admonition.
segments.s669-g73: This clause expresses a future negation with a predicative phrase. نخواهی شد = نخواهد شد (archaic/flexible word order) ‘will not become’ (here functioning as future auxiliary); از خون مردم is an ablative/comitative phrase ‘with/from the blood of the people’; تو is the 2nd-person pronoun placed before the predicate sِیر ‘sated/satiety’. Together: ‘You will not become sated from the people’s blood.’
segments.s669-g74: برآنم که is a formula meaning ‘I am of the opinion that / I hold that’. هستی تو = ‘your being / your nature’ (هستی used as ‘being’/‘state’), and درنده شیر is a nominal phrase ‘predatory lion’ (درنده qualifies شیر). The clause states the speaker’s judgment: ‘I think that you are a predatory lion.’
segments.s669-g75: This is a nominal cluster identifying a person: بریده سر is an epithet (‘cut‑off head’), followed by ساوه شاه ‘the king of Saveh’. آنک is the relative/particle ‘who/that’, and مهر is the noun ‘affection / love’. Together this introduces ‘the king of Saveh, (nicknamed) “Cut‑head”, who had affection…’.
segments.s669-g76: مهر برو داشت = مهر بر او داشت (classical contraction) ‘he had love/affection toward him’; داشت is the past imperfective ‘had/kept’. تا بود گردان سپهر is a temporal clause ‘until / so long as the revolving heavens existed’ (تا introduces duration, بود = ‘was/existed’, گردان سپهر = ‘the turning/revolving sky’ i.e. the heavens).
segments.s669-g77: سپاهی is the force/army (object/topic); بران گونه is an adverbial phrase ‘in that manner / in that sort’; کردی is 2nd‑person past ‘you did’; تباه is a predicate/adjective ‘ruined/destroyed’. Word order is object + manner + verb + result adjective: ‘You made the army ruined in that way.’
segments.s669-g78: که introduces a result or explanatory clause ‘so that / to the extent that’. بخشایش (forgiveness/mercy) is the object, آورد is past ‘brought/caused to come’, and خورشید و ماه are the agents/subjects ‘the sun and the moon’. The clause literal: ‘that the sun and moon brought forgiveness’ — a hyperbolic image of extreme ruin and mercy.
segments.s669-g79: ازان = از آن ‘from that/of that’; شاه جنگی = ‘that warlike king’ (شاه modified by جنگی). منم is the emphatic/poetic form of ‘I am’. یادگار is the predicative noun ‘memento / souvenir / trophy’. The phrase therefore reads: ‘I am a memento (of war) from that warlike king.’
segments.s669-g80: مرا هم (written here together) = ‘me also’; چنان دان is an imperative/command ‘consider (me) thus’ (دان = consider/know). که introduces the comparative or explanatory clause; کشتی = ‘ship’; بزار (imperative/causative) = ‘put/place / set (afloat)’ (here idiomatic: treat me as you would a ship you launch). Overall: ‘Consider me also in the same way — as a ship you put/launch.’
segments.s669-g89: من is the 1st-person subject pronoun. ایدون شوم is an archaic/poetic periphrasis: ایدون (here an old-form verb/adverb of 'coming/now') together with شوم (1st‑singular subjunctive/optative of شدن) expresses the speaker's intention/future action ('I shall now go/come'). سوی is a preposition 'toward'. خرگاه is the place-word (here 'court/lowly dwelling' in older Persian) and خوی + ش is the possessive sequence with the enclitic -ش marking 3rd‑person possession ('his/her/its').
segments.s669-g90: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one'. بازجویم here functions as a first‑person verb form (classical present/subjunctive/intentional) built on بازجو‑, meaning 'I will seek/inquire' (in poetry nouns and verbs sometimes look alike). سر راه is an idiom 'on the road / along the way' with سر = 'head/top' + راه = 'way'. خوی + ش again gives the 3rd‑person possessive enclitic attached to 'خوی'.
segments.s669-g91: نویسم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive (classical/poetic) of نوشتن used to express the speaker's intention 'I will write' or 'let me write'. یکی نامه is 'a certain letter' (یکی = indefinite). زی is an archaic/prepositional particle used with names of persons in poetry (here functioning as the marker 'to/for'), and شهریار is the addressee 'the king'.
segments.s669-g92: مگر here introduces a wish/hope or contingency ('hopefully / that perhaps'); ز + و is the poetic sequence meaning 'from/through it/him' where ز = 'from' and و is the 3rd‑person clitic (spelled separately in the word list). شوم is again 1st‑person subjunctive 'I may become', and ایمن از روزگار is the predicate 'safe/protected from fate/fortune', with از + روزگار marking source/thing being avoided.
segments.s669-g93: گر‌ای is the conditional particle گر 'if' plus the vocative ای (address), so the phrase addresses 'O base one' while introducing a conditional. دون is the adjective 'base/low'. که is the relative/linking particle 'who/that'. اندر means 'in/within', and پذیرد is 3rd‑person (present/subjunctive/indicative) of پذیرفتن 'accept, admit'. م here represents the clitic pronoun 'me' split from را in the word list (the intended object is 'مرا'), and را is the definite object marker attached to that object.
segments.s669-g94: ازین is از + این 'from this' (here 'from this making/act'). ساختن is the infinitive/noun 'making/doing' used abstractly. پس means 'after/then'. گزیرد is a 3rd‑person form expressing leaving/escaping/turning away (the exact nuance is contextually determined: 'he will abandon/turn away'); م + را again represent the object 'me' (مرا) with را the object marker.
segments.s669-g95: من is the subject 'I'. آن بارگه is 'that court/palace' (آن = that, بارگه = royal court). رایکی is a modest qualifier ('small/mere' or 'trivial' in sense—a humbling word that softens the self‑description). بنده‌ام is بنده + ـام 'I am a servant', with the enclitic -ام marking 1st‑person.
segments.s669-g96: دل is fronted as the topic 'my heart'. از مهتری = 'from (desire for) preeminence' (از = from, مهتری = preeminence/chiefly honors). پاک here means 'clean/pure' or 'completely', and برکنده‌ام is the perfect/resultative first‑person 'I have torn off / detached', so the line says the heart has been cleanly detached from ambition for rank.
segments.s669-g129: مگر here is the exception‑marker 'except/except for'; نامدار is used as an epithet (and likely a proper name) immediately after it; از + دز is the classical 'out of the fort' (از = 'from/out of', دز = older spelling for دژ, 'fort/stronghold'); آید برون is a verb phrase with آید 'comes' and برون 'out/forth' following it (verb then directional adverb), so the segment reads 'except Namdār comes out of the fort.'
segments.s669-g130: چوبیند is a poetic/archaic past plural meaning 'they beat/struck (with clubs)'; the sentence then gives the result: همه دشت را ('the entire plain' with را marking the definite/affected object) رود خون ('a river of blood' — رود as 'river' and خون 'blood'). Word order is typical poetic Persian: verb (چوبیند) + result phrase, so 'they struck, and the whole plain became a river of blood.'
segments.s669-g131: ببد is an archaic/past copular verb meaning 'it was' or 'there was.' بر در دز = 'at/on the gate of the fort' (بر = on/upon, در = gate/door, دز = fort); ازین سان is a fixed phrase 'in this way/thus' (ازین = 'this', سان = 'manner/so'); سه روز states the duration 'three days' following the verb phrase (no additional preposition is required in Persian to mark duration).
segments.s669-g132: چهارم 'on the fourth (day)' is a temporal noun in front of a subordinate temporal clause introduced by چو 'when'; چو here means 'when.' بفروخت is past-tense 'he sold/parted with' (verb first, as is common in classical/poetic narrative), and گیتی فروز is a compound epithet 'Giti‑froz / world‑brightener' (functioning as the actor/subject after the verb in V–S order). The clause is elliptical and continues the narrative ('on the fourth, when Giti‑froz did X ...').
segments.s669-g133: پیامی فرستاد is 'he sent a message' (پیامی = 'a message', فرستاد = 'sent'). پرموده را is a person phrase marked by را, meaning 'he sent Par‑mudeh (the experienced one)' — in older Persian it is common to use را with a person when dispatching someone as an object (i.e., 'he sent Par‑mudeh [off] with a message').
segments.s669-g134: This segment names the recipients/vocatives of the message. آن مهتر کشور = 'that/chief of the realm' (آن = 'that/the', مهتر = 'chief/headman', کشور = 'country/realm'). The initial مر is an archaic particle introducing the addressees/message direction (not the modern 'me'); دوده را is a second name or element added by the poet. The whole segment functions as the indirect object/target of the sending: 'to that chief of the realm and to Dūd(eh).' (Note: مر and دوده here are archaic/onomastic usages; modern dictionaries' basic senses may not capture the vocative/particle use.)
segments.s669-g135: که‌ای is an archaic vocative/exclamatory opening (here addressing someone: 'O ...'); مهتر و شاه ترکان چین = 'chief and king of the Turks of China' (مهتر = 'chief', و = 'and', شاه = 'king', ترکان = 'the Turks', چین = 'China'). Word order places the titles together as the addressee.
segments.s669-g136: زگیتی is an adverbial phrase 'from the world' or 'in the world' (ز = 'from/of' + گیتی = 'world'), چرا کردی = 'why did you do' (چرا = 'why', کردی = second‑person past 'you did'), این دز گزین = 'choose this thief' (این = 'this', دز = 'thief' (dialectal/poetic), گزین = infinitive/verb 'to choose' or participial idea 'chosen'). The clause is a direct question: 'Why in the world did you choose this thief?'.
segments.s669-g17: فرستاد is the simple past third-person singular 'he sent/dispatched' (subject is omitted and understood from context). تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that / in order that'), so the following clause supplies the intended action. گرد بر گرد باغ is a compact prepositional phrase: گرد (poetic 'around') + بر (preposition) + گرد (repetition/intensifier 'around') + باغ 'garden' — together 'around the garden', and it syntactically belongs to the subordinate purpose clause whose verb appears next.
segments.s669-g18: بگیرند is the third-person plural present-subjunctive (or jussive) of گرفتن used inside the تا-purpose clause to express the intended action 'that they seize/take'. گردنکشان is the plural agent/object 'rebels' (those who pull necks = insurgents) and is the direct object of بگیرند. بی‌چراغ is a postpositive adjective phrase 'without lamps/torches' modifying گردنکشان; together: 'seize the rebels who are without lamps.'
segments.s669-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بهرام is the subject. آگه شد is a two-word predicate meaning 'became aware / learned' (آگه 'aware' + شد 'became'). از introduces the object of awareness 'about/concerning', and کارشان 'their affair/deed' is what he learned about.
segments.s669-g20: ز (poetic form of از) here continues the 'about/of' sense and introduces the particulars of what he learned. رای is 'counsel/plan' modified by جهانجوی ('world‑seeking' / ambitious) — a compound-like modifier giving 'the ambitious/world-seeking counsel' — and و بازارشان adds 'and their bazaar/market' as another thing he learned about. The whole phrase is a continuation/clarification of 'از کارشان'.
segments.s669-g21: یلان is the subject 'the warriors' (they who fight). سینه را گفت is a poetic idiom: literally 'spoke the breast', i.e., 'cried out from their chests / spoke wholeheartedly' (سینه را here is an adverbial/idiomatic phrase marked by را). گَفت is the past 'said' and is immediately followed by the vocative کای 'O', addressing someone directly. سرافراز is the epithet/noun in the vocative 'O exalted/honored one'.
segments.s669-g22: بدیوار باغ اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning 'against the inner wall of the garden' (بد- as the prefix + دیوار 'wall', then باغ 'garden', اندرون 'inner/inside'). رخنه ساز is a terse imperative construction: رخنه 'breach' + ساز (here functioning as 'make!' — an imperative sense common in poetic register), so the command reads 'make a breach'. Word order places the location first and the imperative last.
segments.s669-g23: پس آنگاه is a redundant temporal pair 'then / afterwards' setting the time. بهرام و ایزد گشسب is the coordinated subject 'Bahram and Izad‑Gushasp' (ایزد here forms part of the hero's name but is etymologically 'god/deity'). The names are simply conjoined with و 'and'.
segments.s669-g24: نشستند is the past third-person plural 'they sat / they mounted' (here idiomatically 'mounted' or 'took their seats on horses'). با جنگجویان 'with the warriors' uses با 'with' to mark accompaniment. بر اسب 'on horses' (بر + اسب) indicates the place/instrument of sitting — 'on horseback'. The whole segment says they mounted up together with the warriors.

sentences.s669-s21: When half of the dark night had passed, the war commander went out onto the plain.
sentences.s669-s22: The spahbed drew his army toward that side, but no one of the Turkish vanguard saw him.
sentences.s669-s23: When he came near the battlefield, the sound of an iron reed (flute) rose from the road.
sentences.s669-s24: When the drum's call came and the trumpet-blasts sounded, the warlike Turks leapt from their places.
